title: Controlled Loop Expansion for the Topological Heavy Fermion Model
summary: We develop a controlled theoretical framework for the topological heavy fermion model relevant to magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene, where low density conduction electrons hybridize with a lattice of strongly interacting f-sites. By tracing out the localized electrons, we derive an effective action for the conduction electrons with long-range in time effective interactions, built from correlators of the single f-site problem. We identify a small hybridization-phase-space parameter resulting in a controlled loop expansion, enabling the derivation of nonperturbative results in either the interaction or the hybridization strength. To tree-level, the results are equivalent to the Hubbard I approximation. At higher loop order, we derive two key results applicable to temperatures above the flavor ordering temperature and below the on-site charging energy: 1) the quasi-particle lifetime, 2) the flavor susceptibility of the system. Remarkably, despite being strongly interacting, we find the susceptibility to accurately obey a Curie-Weiss law parametrically close to the Curie temperature.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a controlled theoretical framework for the topological heavy fermion model in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene, where conduction electrons hybridize with strongly interacting f-sites. The authors derive an effective action with long-range interactions and identify a small hybridization-phase-space parameter enabling a controlled loop expansion. They obtain nonperturbative results including the quasi-particle lifetime and demonstrate that the flavor susceptibility follows a Curie-Weiss law despite strong interactions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a controlled theoretical framework for the topological heavy fermion model relevant to magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene, where low-density conduction electrons hybridize with a lattice of strongly interacting f-sites. This requires understanding nonperturbative effects in either the interaction or hybridization strength.
AI.method: The authors trace out the localized electrons to derive an effective action for conduction electrons with long-range in time effective interactions built from correlators of the single f-site problem. They identify a small hybridization-phase-space parameter resulting in a controlled loop expansion, enabling nonperturbative results. Tree-level results are equivalent to the Hubbard I approximation, with higher loop orders providing quasi-particle lifetime and flavor susceptibility.

Remarkably, despite strong interactions, the susceptibility accurately obeys a Curie-Weiss law parametrically close to the Curie temperature.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a controlled approach to studying strongly correlated electron systems in twisted bilayer graphene, yielding nonperturbative results that reveal the quasi-particle dynamics and magnetic susceptibility behavior, with the susceptibility following Curie-Weiss law even in the strong interaction regime.

title: Divergent spin conductivity on the verge of ferromagnetic quantum criticality
summary: We show that the spin conductivity of a metal approaching a ferromagnetic quantum critical point exhibits divergent fluctuation corrections. This effect arises from critical spin fluctuations and constitutes a spin analog of the Aslamazov-Larkin theory of paraconductivity in superconductors. The spin current is derived in linear response within a Gaussian-level treatment of the effective action for a system with easy-plane magnetic anisotropy. We demonstrate the consistency of our spin transport theory by showing that it (i) fulfills the Ward identity and (ii) yields vanishing spin stiffness in the normal state. The critical enhancement of the spin conductivity is interpreted as incipient spin superfluidity in the quantum critical region. This is further supported by an intuitive picture based on the current-loop representation of the easy-plane ferromagnet.

AI.tldr: The spin conductivity of a metal near a ferromagnetic quantum critical point shows divergent fluctuation corrections due to critical spin fluctuations, analogous to Aslamazov-Larkin paraconductivity in superconductors.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to develop a spin transport theory for systems with easy-plane magnetic anisotropy approaching a ferromagnetic quantum critical point, motivated by the need to understand critical spin fluctuations and their effect on spin conductivity.
AI.method: The spin current is derived in linear response using a Gaussian-level treatment of the effective action for a system with easy-plane magnetic anisotropy.
AI.result: The critical spin fluctuations cause divergent enhancement of spin conductivity. The theory satisfies the Ward identity and yields vanishing spin stiffness in the normal state.
AI.conclusion: The critical enhancement of spin conductivity is interpreted as incipient spin superfluidity in the quantum critical region, supported by a current-loop representation of the easy-plane ferromagnet.

title: Quantum Charge-4e Superconductivity and Deconfined Pseudocriticality in the Attractive SU(4) Hubbard Model
summary: Unlike conventional charge-2e superconductors, a charge-4e superconductor exhibits long-range coherence of electron quartets rather than Cooper pairs. Clear zero-temperature realizations of charge-4e superconductivity remain rare. Here, we investigate the zero-temperature phase diagram of the attractive SU(4) Hubbard model with numerically exact, large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations overcoming major technical hurdles. We identify both charge-2e and charge-4e superconducting phases. Upon increasing interaction, charge-2e correlations are suppressed and eventually vanish, while the charge-4e correlations remain robust and converge with system size, signaling the onset of a quartet-condensed phase. Interestingly, across the charge-2e--charge-4e transition, single electrons remain gapped, while charge-2e correlations exhibit a scaling behavior inconsistent with a conventional Landau description. These features are naturally captured by a fractionalized framework in which the physical charge-2e order parameter is a composite field coupled to an emergent non-Abelian gauge structure. We formulate an Sp(4) gauge-Higgs theory that realizes deconfined quantum pseudocriticality between the Higgs (charge-2e) phase and the confined (charge-4e) phase. The Sp(4) gauge-Higgs theory yields pseudocriticality through a fixed-point collision, and its one-loop collision-point exponents quantitatively track the QMC results. Our results establish charge-4e superconductivity as a bona fide zero-temperature phase, provide a simple model for future studies in a numerically exact framework, and reveal an unconventional route to superconducting criticality.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates charge-4e superconductivity in the attractive SU(4) Hubbard model using quantum Monte Carlo simulations, discovering a phase transition from charge-2e to charge-4e superconductivity where charge-4e correlations remain robust while single electrons stay gapped.
AI.motivation: Unlike conventional charge-2e superconductors with Cooper pairs, charge-4e superconductors exhibit long-range coherence of electron quartets. Clear zero-temperature realizations of this phenomenon are rare, motivating the search for experimentally verifiable models and a fundamental understanding of quartet condensation.
AI.method: Large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the attractive SU(4) Hubbard model, combined with theoretical analysis using a fractionalized framework with emergent non-Abelian gauge structure and an Sp(4) gauge-Higgs theory.
AI.result: The study identifies both charge-2e and charge-4e superconducting phases. Upon increasing interaction, charge-2e correlations are suppressed and eventually vanish, while charge-4e correlations remain robust and converge with system size. The transition exhibits unconventional scaling behavior inconsistent with conventional Landau description, with single electrons remaining gapped across the transition. The Sp(4) gauge-Higgs theory with one-loop collision-point exponents quantitatively tracks the QMC results.
AI.conclusion: Charge-4e superconductivity is established as a bona fide zero-temperature phase. The attractive SU(4) Hubbard model provides a simple platform for future numerically exact studies. The research reveals an unconventional route to superconducting criticality through deconfined quantum pseudocriticality between Higgs (charge-2e) and confined (charge-4e) phases.

title: Limits of validity for Migdal-Eliashberg theory: role of polarons/bi-polarons
summary: It is widely believed that in an adiabatic limit a Fermi liquid state of an electron-phonon system described by Migdal-Eliashberg theory remains stable before a dressed phonon softens. Using Holstein model as a prototypical example and variational/analytic considerations we demonstrate that in a wide range of fillings both in 3D and 2D, a polaronic/bi-polaronic state emerges before phonon softening; at small filling in 3D this happens already at weak coupling. We show that a polaronic/bi-polaronic state emerges, upon increasing coupling, via an intermediate pseudogap-type mixed state, in which some fermions regain Fermi liquid behavior, yet Luttinger theorem is broken. At even larger couplings the density of states gradually approaches its form in the atomic limit.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the conventional belief that Fermi liquid behavior remains stable in electron-phonon systems until phonon softening occurs, using the Holstein model as a prototypical example. The authors demonstrate that polaronic/bipolaronic states emerge before phonon softening in both 2D and 3D systems across a wide range of fillings.
AI.motivation: The prevailing view in Migdal-Eliashberg theory suggests that a Fermi liquid state remains stable in the adiabatic limit until a dressed phonon softens. This work seeks to challenge this assumption by investigating whether alternative ground states emerge before phonon softening occurs.
AI.method: The authors employ variational and analytical considerations using the Holstein model as a prototypical example, examining both 3D and 2D systems across a wide range of fillings.
AI.result: The study finds that polaronic/bipolaronic states emerge before phonon softening in both 3D and 2D across a wide range of fillings. At small fillings in 3D, this occurs even at weak coupling. An intermediate pseudogap-type mixed state appears upon increasing coupling, where some fermions regain Fermi liquid behavior while Luttinger theorem is broken. At even larger couplings, the density of states gradually approaches its atomic limit form.
AI.conclusion: The conventional belief about Fermi liquid stability in electron-phonon systems is challenged. The emergence of polaronic/bipolaronic states precedes phonon softening, with an intermediate pseudogap phase showing broken Luttinger theorem behavior, suggesting a more complex phase diagram than previously assumed.

title: $μ$SR study of time-reversal symmetry constraints and bulk superfluid response in Li$_{0.95}$FeAs
summary: We report zero-field (ZF) and transverse-field (TF) muon-spin rotation/relaxation ($μ$SR) measurements on superconducting Li$_{0.95}$FeAs ($T_{\rm c}\simeq16.0$ K) grown by a high-pressure self-flux method. The ZF-$μ$SR data show no detectable change of the electronic relaxation rate on cooling through $T_{\rm c}$, providing no evidence for time-reversal-symmetry breaking in the superconducting state. TF-$μ$SR measurements reveal a well-developed vortex response with strong flux pinning and a negligible nonsuperconducting contribution, confirming that superconductivity is a bulk property of the sample. From the second moment of the internal field distribution we determine a low-temperature in-plane magnetic penetration depth $λ_{ab}= 245(15)$ nm. The temperature dependence of the normalized superfluid density is well described by an effective two-gap model with $Δ_1 = 2.0(2)$ meV and $Δ_2 = 0.7(2)$ meV. A quantitative comparison with ARPES-based band weights shows that the $μ$SR response is dominated by the Fermi-surface sheets carrying the intermediate and small superconducting gaps, whereas the band hosting the largest gap contributes only about 3\% to the total superfluid density and is therefore not resolved in the present analysis. Taken together, these results establish Li$_{0.95}$FeAs as a bulk multigap superconductor without detectable time-reversal symmetry breaking and show how $μ$SR reconciles the gap scales reported by bulk and surface-sensitive probes in this multiband system.

title: Configuration-dependent electronic and optical properties of 2D Mo$_{1-x}$W$_x$S$_2$ alloys across the full composition range
summary: Here we analyze multiple symmetry-inequivalent atomic configurations across the entire composition range of the isovalent and isostructural Mo$_x$W$_{1-x}$S$_2$ alloy using density-functional theory and Monte Carlo simulations. Our results show that although structural stability and energetics are largely composition-driven, the electronic and optical properties exhibit configuration dependence, with local atomic arrangements critically shaping band-edge splitting, valley structure, effective-mass anisotropy, and optical selection rules. In contrast to the pristine monolayers, even in the absence of spin-orbit coupling (SOC), splitting of the band edges at the $K$ point is observed across the entire composition range. In particular, while the valence-band maximum (VBM) remains largely robust, the conduction-band minimum (CBM) shows strong configuration-dependent splitting from few meV up to hundredths of meV. This behaviour leads to a non-trivial dependence of the valley energetics. Configurations with well-separated conduction bands support additional optically active transitions beyond the conventional A and B excitons in MoS$_2$ and WS$_2$ monolayers, whereas nearly degenerate cases exhibit a reduced number of allowed transitions, observed for specific configurations at $x = 1/3$ and $x = 2/3$. These results demonstrate that the number and character of optically active transitions are governed not only by composition, but also by the microscopic arrangement of atoms. Moreover, we found the hole effective masses at the VBM show configuration-dependent anisotropy, reflecting sensitivity to local symmetry breaking and implying direction-dependent transport.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes Mo_xW_{1-x}S_2 alloys across the entire composition range using DFT and Monte Carlo simulations, revealing that while structural stability is composition-driven, electronic and optical properties critically depend on local atomic arrangements, affecting band-edge splitting, valley structure, effective-mass anisotropy, and optical selection rules.
AI.motivation: Understand how local atomic arrangements in isovalent isostructural alloys affect electronic and optical properties beyond simple composition effects.
AI.method: Density-functional theory (DFT) and Monte Carlo simulations were used to analyze multiple symmetry-inequivalent atomic configurations across the entire composition range.
AI.result: Even without spin-orbit coupling, band-edge splitting at the K point is observed across the entire composition range. The valence-band maximum remains robust while the conduction-band minimum shows strong configuration-dependent splitting (few meV to hundredths of meV). Configurations with well-separated conduction bands support additional optically active transitions beyond conventional A and B excitons, while nearly degenerate cases at x=1/3 and x=2/3 exhibit reduced allowed transitions. Hole effective masses show configuration-dependent anisotropy.
AI.conclusion: The number and character of optically active transitions in Mo_xW_{1-x}S_2 alloys are governed not only by composition but also by the microscopic arrangement of atoms, with implications for direction-dependent transport and optical device applications.

title: Breakdown of the Migdal-Eliashberg theory for electron-phonon systems. Role of polarons/bi-polarons
summary: The Migdal-Eliashberg theory (MET) describes electrons interacting with phonons in the adiabatic limit when the phonon Debye frequency is much smaller than the Fermi energy. A conventional belief is that MET holds even at strong coupling, when electron self-energy is large, and breaks down only near the point where the dressed phonon spectrum softens to near zero. We analyze numerically and analytically a different option -- collapse to a polaronic/bipolaronic ground state. The last scenario has never been analyzed in precise quantitative terms for a generic electron density. Using variational considerations, we establish rigorous upper bounds on the coupling $λ$, at which a FL state transforms into the bipolaron/polaron state. We show that at small and near-maximum densities, this happens well before a dressed phonon softens. This is true both in 2D and 3D systems; in the latter the upper bound on $λ$ tends to zero in the limit of small or near-full density. We present analytical reasoning for this behavior based on hints extracted from exact diagrammatic treatment of the on-site Holstein model for the spin polarized case and argue that polarons are produced by fermions with energies comparable to the bandwidth; i.e., polaron formation is outside the realm of MET. Closer to half-filling, the leading instability upon increasing $λ$ is towards a charge-density-wave state (CDW), and there exists a strong coupling regime of MET near this instability, while the polaron/bipolaron state develops at larger $λ$ out of a CDW-ordered state and inherits a CDW order over some range of coupling.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the conventional understanding of Migdal-Eliashberg theory (MET) by investigating whether strong electron-phonon coupling leads to polaron/bipolaron formation rather than just phonon softening. The authors establish rigorous upper bounds on the coupling strength λ at which a Fermi liquid transforms into a polaronic/bipolaronic state.
AI.motivation: The conventional belief holds that MET remains valid even at strong coupling (when electron self-energy is large) and only breaks down near phonon spectrum softening. This paper explores an alternative scenario: collapse to polaronic/bipolaronic ground states, which has never been quantitatively analyzed for generic electron densities.
AI.method: The authors use variational considerations to establish rigorous upper bounds on coupling λ, combined with analytical reasoning from exact diagrammatic treatment of the on-site Holstein model for the spin-polarized case. The analysis covers both 2D and 3D systems.
AI.result: The study finds that at small and near-maximum densities, the FL to polaron/bipolaron transition occurs well before dressed phonon softening. This holds in both 2D and 3D, with the 3D upper bound on λ tending to zero at small or near-full densities. Near half-filling, the leading instability is toward charge-density-wave (CDW) state, with polaron/bipolaron formation developing out of CDW-ordered state at larger coupling.
AI.conclusion: Polaron formation involves fermions with energies comparable to the bandwidth, placing it outside the realm of MET. This work demonstrates that MET has a more limited domain of validity than previously believed, particularly in the strong coupling regime where polaron physics takes over.

title: Revisiting apparent ideal diamagnetism at ambient conditions in graphene-n-heptane-permalloy systems
summary: We previously reported apparent ideal diamagnetism at ambient conditions in a graphene-n-heptane-permalloy system. At the same time, the experiments revealed inconsistent behavior, including signal freezing and occasional paramagnetic responses. Further measurements performed without graphene produced similar signals, indicating that graphene is not responsible for the observed effects. The results suggest that magnetic field redistribution caused by inhomogeneities in the permalloy foil and experimental geometry can mimic ideal diamagnetism in sub-milligauss measurements. These findings revise the interpretation of our earlier results and emphasize caution in interpreting ultra-low-field magnetic measurements.

AI.tldr: This paper revises the authors' earlier claim of observing ideal diamagnetism in a graphene-n-heptane-permalloy system. Through control experiments without graphene, they discovered that the observed effects were actually artifacts caused by magnetic field redistribution from inhomogeneities in the permalloy foil and experimental geometry.
AI.motivation: The original study reported apparent ideal diamagnetism but showed inconsistent behavior (signal freezing, occasional paramagnetic responses), raising questions about the validity of the finding.
AI.method: Control experiments without graphene in the system, testing whether graphene was responsible for the observed magnetic signals.
AI.result: Similar signals were observed even without graphene, demonstrating that graphene was NOT responsible for the apparent diamagnetic effects. The effects were actually caused by magnetic field redistribution due to inhomogeneities in the permalloy foil and experimental geometry.
AI.conclusion: The original interpretation was incorrect - what was observed was an artifact of the measurement setup, not a genuine property of the graphene system. This highlights the importance of caution when interpreting ultra-low-field magnetic measurements.

title: Ultra-high-vacuum cluster tool for epitaxial synthesis and optical spectroscopy of reactive 2D materials
summary: The large-area synthesis of high-crystalline-quality two-dimensional (2D) materials is at the core of novel material integration for semiconductor technology. This effort relies on developing fabrication and characterization techniques that can uncover the material's intrinsic properties by preserving its pristine conditions. In this article, we present an all ultra-high-vacuum cluster for the growth using molecular beam epitaxy of 2D semiconductors that are unstable under ambient conditions and optical spectroscopy using low temperature (20 K) photoluminescence and Raman scattering. The optical chamber of the setup provides micrometer scale spatial resolution and the ability to scan the entire wafer. The performance of its setup regarding spatial resolution, temperature control over a temperature range of 20-300 K using a closed-cycle cryostat and long-term preservation are demonstrated using as-grown post-transition metal monochalcogenides. Furthermore, we introduce a deconvolution-based algorithm to recover spatial information under vibration using a system-specific point-spread function. This enables in situ analysis of the structural and optoelectronic properties of as-grown materials in their pristine form, providing rich and reproducible feedback for both fundamental studies and the optimization of scalable 2D material growth toward integration in advanced devices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an all ultra-high-vacuum cluster tool system combining molecular beam epitaxy for growing unstable 2D semiconductors with in situ low-temperature (20 K) optical spectroscopy (photoluminescence and Raman scattering). The system features micrometer-scale spatial resolution, wafer-scale scanning, and temperature control from 20-300 K. A deconvolution algorithm is also introduced to correct for vibration artifacts.
AI.motivation: The large-area synthesis of high-crystalline-quality 2D materials requires fabrication and characterization techniques that preserve pristine conditions, especially for materials unstable under ambient conditions. There was a need for an integrated system that can grown and characterize these sensitive materials without exposure to air.
AI.method: The authors developed an ultra-high-vacuum cluster tool integrating molecular beam epitaxy for growth with low-temperature photoluminescence and Raman scattering for optical characterization. They used a closed-cycle cryostat for temperature control (20-300 K) and demonstrated spatial resolution across entire wafers. A deconvolution-based algorithm using system-specific point-spread function was developed to recover spatial information under vibration conditions.
AI.result: The system successfully demonstrated in situ analysis of as-grown post-transition metal monochalcogenides, preserving their pristine conditions. The deconvolution algorithm enables recovery of spatial information despite vibration artifacts. The setup provides rich and reproducible feedback for fundamental studies and optimization of scalable 2D material growth.
AI.conclusion: This integrated UHV cluster tool enables the growth and characterization of air-sensitive 2D semiconductors in their pristine form, providing a powerful platform for fundamental studies and device integration optimization of 2D materials.

title: Topologically non-trivial gap function and topology-induced time-reversal symmetry breaking in a superconductor with singular dynamical interaction
summary: In many strongly correlated electron systems, non-Fermi liquid behavior and unconventional superconductivity can be viewed as emerging from an effective 4-fermion interaction with a singular frequency dependence. A pairing instability in such a system is qualitatively different from that in a Fermi liquid and generally gives rise to multiple pairing states with topologically distinct gap functions. However, in the systems studied so far, a topologically trivial solution has the lowest energy. Here we show that a repulsive Hubbard-type interaction with a finite cutoff added to a model with a singular dynamical interaction selects, in some parameter range, the theretofore subleading, topologically nontrivial solution. We consider a minimal model that displays this behavior and show that the transformation between the topologically trivial and nontrivial gap functions necessarily occurs via an intermediate phase with topology-induced breaking of time-reversal symmetry.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates superconductivity in strongly correlated electron systems with singular dynamical interactions. It demonstrates that topologically nontrivial pairing states can become energetically favored over trivial ones when a repulsive Hubbard-type interaction with finite cutoff is included. The transition between topologically trivial and nontrivial gap functions occurs via an intermediate phase exhibiting topology-induced time-reversal symmetry breaking.
AI.motivation: In strongly correlated electron systems displaying non-Fermi liquid behavior and unconventional superconductivity, previous studies found that topologically trivial pairing solutions always had the lowest energy. The motivation was to discover conditions under which the previously subleading topologically nontrivial solutions could become the energetically preferred state.
AI.method: The authors constructed a minimal model incorporating a repulsive Hubbard-type interaction with a finite cutoff added to a model with singular dynamical interaction. They analyzed the parameter space to study the competition between topologically distinct gap functions and examined the transformation pathways between different topological states.
AI.result: The finite cutoff in the repulsive interaction selects the topologically nontrivial solution in certain parameter ranges,逆转ing the typical energetics where trivial solutions dominate. The transformation between trivial and nontrivial gap functions necessarily occurs through an intermediate phase with topology-induced breaking of time-reversal symmetry.
AI.conclusion: This work shows that topologically nontrivial superconducting states can emerge as the ground state in correlated electron systems under specific conditions involving repulsive interactions with finite cutoffs, challenging the conventional wisdom that trivial states are always favored. This provides a new mechanism for realizing topological superconductivity in real materials.

title: Direct laser micromachining of superconducting terahertz Josephson plasma emitters
summary: We demonstrate a rapid, maskless fabrication method for superconducting terahertz Josephson plasma emitters (JPEs) based on direct ultraviolet laser micromachining of Bi$_2$Sr$_2$CaCu$_2$O$_{8+δ}$ (Bi-2212) single crystals. Although machining debris is formed near the processed regions, uniform stacks of intrinsic Josephson junctions are preserved inside the crystal, enabling stable terahertz emission. Devices fabricated with Ag, Cu, and Cr electrodes all exhibited terahertz radiation, with Cu electrodes showing performance comparable to Ag while offering a low-cost alternative. Spectroscopic and polarization analyses indicate that the emitted radiation is elliptically polarized and dominated by the geometrical cavity resonance mode. Structural and electrical characterizations reveal that the machining width and depth are not limited by the optical spot size but are governed by the anisotropic thermal conductivity of Bi-2212, consistent with a thermally dominated laser ablation process. This direct laser micromachining approach provides a fast and versatile fabrication technique for JPEs and is broadly applicable to superconducting electronics and terahertz devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates a rapid, maskless fabrication method for superconducting terahertz Josephson plasma emitters (JPEs) using direct ultraviolet laser micromachining of Bi-2212 single crystals. The technique preserves intrinsic Josephson junctions while enabling stable THz emission, with Cu electrodes offering a low-cost alternative to Ag.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a fast, versatile fabrication technique for JPEs that overcomes limitations of existing methods. Traditional fabrication approaches are typically time-consuming and require masks, while this work aims to provide a maskless, rapid alternative that enables practical terahertz device production.
AI.method: The method involves direct ultraviolet laser micromachining of Bi-2212 single crystals to fabricate JPEs. Three different electrode materials (Ag, Cu, and Cr) were tested. The process was characterized through spectroscopic and polarization analyses, along with structural and electrical characterizations to understand the machining parameters and their effects.
AI.result: The key results show that: (1) uniform stacks of intrinsic Josephson junctions are preserved despite some machining debris; (2) all three electrode materials (Ag, Cu, Cr) exhibited terahertz radiation; (3) Cu electrodes perform comparably to Ag while being more cost-effective; (4) the emitted radiation is elliptically polarized and dominated by geometrical cavity resonance mode; (5) machining dimensions are governed by the anisotropic thermal conductivity of Bi-2212, consistent with a thermally dominated laser ablation process.
AI.conclusion: The direct laser micromachining approach provides a fast and versatile fabrication technique for JPEs. This method is broadly applicable to superconducting electronics and terahertz devices, offering a practical solution for manufacturing terahertz emitters with reduced cost and processing time.

title: Environment-dependent tight-binding models from ab initio pseudo-atomic orbital Hamiltonians
summary: \textit{Ab initio} pseudo-atomic orbital (PAO) Hamiltonians express the electronic structure of a solid in a compact, localized basis that spans the same Hilbert space as a conventional Slater--Koster tight-binding model, thereby providing an exact \textit{ab initio} representation without any loss of accuracy. Building on this correspondence, we develop an environment-dependent tight-binding (EDTB) framework in which Slater--Koster hopping integrals are augmented with bond-screening functions that capture the local coordination environment. All parameters are determined by fitting to the PAO eigenvalue spectrum across multiple atomic configurations simultaneously, which breaks the degeneracy between screening and hopping parameters and yields physically meaningful, transferable models capable of generating Hamiltonians for large systems with \textit{ab initio} precision. We demonstrate the efficiency and accuracy of the approach on four prototypical systems: bulk platinum, silicon surfaces, Si/Ge~[001] superlattices, and twisted bilayer graphene with up to $4{,}324$ atoms. The method is implemented in the \paoflow{} code and integrates seamlessly with its full post-processing suite, enabling the evaluation of a broad range of electronic, optical, and transport properties.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an environment-dependent tight-binding (EDTB) framework that augments Slater-Koster hopping integrals with bond-screening functions to capture local coordination environment. The method is fitted to PAO eigenvalue spectra across multiple atomic configurations, enabling ab initio precision for large systems up to 4,324 atoms.
AI.motivation: Create physically meaningful, transferable models that break the degeneracy between screening and hopping parameters, enabling generation of Hamiltonians for large systems with ab initio precision while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.method: Builds on ab initio pseudo-atomic orbital (PAO) Hamiltonians which span the same Hilbert space as conventional Slater-Koster tight-binding models. The EDTB framework augments Slater-Koster hopping integrals with bond-screening functions that capture local coordination environment. Parameters are determined by fitting to PAO eigenvalue spectra across multiple atomic configurations simultaneously.
AI.result: Demonstrated on four prototypical systems: bulk platinum, silicon surfaces, Si/Ge [001] superlattices, and twisted bilayer graphene with up to 4,324 atoms. The method enables evaluation of electronic, optical, and transport properties.
AI.conclusion: The method is implemented in the paoflow code and integrates seamlessly with its full post-processing suite, providing an efficient and accurate approach for large-scale electronic structure calculations with ab initio precision.

title: Twisted Bilayer Graphene Lifetimes At Integer Fillings: An Analytic Result
summary: Twisted bilayer graphene near integer fillings hosts correlated single-particle excitations whose dispersion and linewidth are increasingly accessible experimentally. We study these excitations using the topological heavy-fermion model, which captures both strong correlations and band topology of twisted bilayer graphene. In the decoupled limit, where both the single-particle fc hybridization and the Hund coupling between f and c electrons are absent, the model admits exact solutions in which free Dirac fermions coexist with interacting f electrons that form zero-width Hubbard bands. By treating the fc hybridization and Hund coupling perturbatively around this solvable limit, we obtain analytical results for the single-particle self-energy. From the resulting self-energy, we derive explicit expressions for both dispersion renormalization and scattering rates of both Hubbard-band excitations and low-energy Dirac modes, thereby establishing an analytical framework for understanding correlated excitations in twisted bilayer graphene. We analyze the scattering of the two kinds, Gamma3 and Gamma1,2, of Dirac electrons and find that they arise from different mechanisms. We also briefly investigate the effect of strain. Finally, we compare these analytical expressions with DMFT results for the same model.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an analytical framework using the topological heavy-fermion model to study correlated excitations in twisted bilayer graphene, deriving explicit expressions for dispersion renormalization and scattering rates that match DMFT results.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the need to understand correlated single-particle excitations in twisted bilayer graphene near integer fillings, which are increasingly accessible experimentally but lack complete analytical theoretical description.
AI.method: The authors use a topological heavy-fermion model and treat fc hybridization and Hund coupling perturbatively around a solvable decoupled limit where free Dirac fermions coexist with interacting f electrons forming zero-width Hubbard bands.
AI.result: The authors obtain analytical expressions for single-particle self-energy, leading to explicit results for dispersion renormalization and scattering rates of both Hubbard-band excitations and low-energy Dirac modes. They find that Gamma3 and Gamma1,2 Dirac electrons arise from different scattering mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes an analytical framework for understanding correlated excitations in twisted bilayer graphene, providing explicit expressions for dispersion renormalization and scattering rates. The comparison with DMFT results validates the perturbative approach and offers insights into the different scattering mechanisms for Gamma3 and Gamma1,2 Dirac electrons.

title: Wide-field magnetic imaging of shielding-current-driven vortex rearrangement under local heating using diamond quantum sensors
summary: Understanding and controlling vortex motion in superconductors are important both for suppressing dissipation in superconducting devices and for device applications that exploit vortices. In this work, we quantitatively imaged the stray magnetic field distribution of vortices in an NbN thin film by wide-field magnetic imaging using a perfectly aligned diamond NV ensemble. By continuously measuring while stepwise varying the applied magnetic field under local laser heating, we captured a rearrangement of the vortex configuration in real space and in real time over more than 100 min. The observed vortex rearrangement is consistent with a reduction of the pinning force due to local laser heating and with the Lorentz force exerted by shielding currents induced by the field variation. These results provide insight into vortex dynamics and suggest potential applications, including vortex exclusion from sensitive regions of superconducting devices and vortex positioning in vortex-based devices.

AI.tldr: This work uses wide-field magnetic imaging with a diamond NV ensemble to quantitatively image vortex motion in an NbN superconductor. The researchers captured real-time vortex rearrangement over 100+ minutes under combined laser heating and applied magnetic field, revealing the interplay between reduced pinning forces and Lorentz forces from shielding currents.
AI.motivation: Understanding and controlling vortex motion in superconductors is crucial for both suppressing dissipation in superconducting devices and enabling vortex-based device applications.
AI.method: Wide-field magnetic imaging using a perfectly aligned diamond NV ensemble was employed to quantitatively image stray magnetic field distribution of vortices. Experiments involved continuous measurement during stepwise variation of applied magnetic field under local laser heating.
AI.result: The researchers successfully captured vortex rearrangement in real space and real time over more than 100 minutes. The observed behavior is consistent with reduced pinning force due to local laser heating and Lorentz force from shielding currents induced by field variation.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide valuable insight into vortex dynamics and suggest potential applications including vortex exclusion from sensitive regions of superconducting devices and precise vortex positioning in vortex-based devices.

title: An Investigation in the Kinetic Persistence of TiO$_2$ Polymorphs using Machine Learning Driven Pathfinding in Crystal Configuration Space
summary: As the number of theoretically predicted materials continues to grow, it becomes increasingly important to assess not only their thermodynamic stability but also their kinetic viability under realistic synthesis conditions. In this study, we investigate the hypothesis that the kinetic persistence of a metastable polymorph is related to the topography of the potential energy landscape separating it from lower energy phases. To accomplish this, we develop a new method for identifying diffusionless transformation pathways between metastable polymorphs and their ground-state counterparts and discuss the energetics of those pathways with respect to the experimental observation of each phase. This algorithm is underpinned by the recently developed Crystal Normal Form, which provides a graph representation of crystal configuration space and supplies the substrate for our pathfinding algorithm. We apply this method to the titanium dioxide system which contains the well-known anatase, rutile, and brookite phases in addition to a number of hypothetical metastable polymorphs.
