title: A Generalized Coherent State Framework for Many-Body Density of States
summary: We develop a general framework to calculate the many-body density of states (DOS) of isolated and interacting quantum systems. Based on the generalized coherent state formalism and the Simon-Lieb bounds for a quantum partition function, our method provides a general method of calculation for the DOS in high-dimensional irreducible sectors. This framework further provides rigorous bounds for the ground state energy in each sector and enables the calculation of microcanonical observables across the entire spectrum. Using the Lipkin-Meshkov-Glick (LMG) model as a test bed, we validate our framework by successfully identifying quantum phase transitions (QPTs) and excited-state quantum phase transitions (ESQPTs) across spin sectors. Unlike existing model-specific numerical or analytical techniques, our formalism relies on general underlying symmetries, making it broadly applicable. Applying our method to the ferromagnetic transverse field Ising chain with power law interactions, we demonstrate that its highest-spin-sector DOS is qualitatively identical to that of LMG-type Hamiltonians. Our work establishes a versatile and computationally efficient bridge between algebraic structure and many-body thermodynamics.

AI.tldr: A general framework for calculating many-body density of states (DOS) of quantum systems using generalized coherent state formalism and Simon-Lieb bounds, applicable across high-dimensional irreducible sectors with rigorous bounds for ground state energy and microcanonical observables.
AI.motivation: The lack of general methods for calculating DOS across quantum systems, with existing techniques being model-specific and limited in scope.
AI.method: Based on generalized coherent state formalism and Simon-Lieb bounds for quantum partition function, providing rigorous bounds for ground state energy in each sector and enabling calculation of microcanonical observables.
AI.result: Successfully identified quantum phase transitions (QPTs) and excited-state quantum phase transitions (ESQPTs) across spin sectors in LMG model; demonstrated highest-spin-sector DOS of ferromagnetic transverse field Ising chain with power law interactions is qualitatively identical to LMG-type Hamiltonians.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a versatile and computationally efficient bridge between algebraic structure and many-body thermodynamics, broadly applicable due to reliance on general underlying symmetries rather than model-specific techniques.

title: Quantum Landscape of Superconducting Diodes
summary: This study maps the quantum landscape of superconducting diodes (SDs) \cite{nadeem23} onto the quantum technology architecture, which is currently constrained by fundamental challenges in control and scalability. In the existing non-integrated quantum technology hardware, control and scalability related issues emerge at two fronts: First, nonlinear and nonreciprocal circuit elements, which are essential building blocks for quantum processors, are often complex, bulky, and dissipative. Second, the temperature gradient between classical control electronics ($T_C\gtrsim$ K), which is also dissipative, and the quantum processor at cryogenic temperatures ($T_Q\sim$ mK) makes scalability even more challenging. The main focus is to reveal how the built-in nonlinearity, nonreciprocity, and quantum functionalities of SDs are significant for on-chip integrated circuit quantum electrodynamics (c-QED), enabling scalable integration of noise-resilient qubit and qubit-interfaces for efficient power delivery, coherent control and memory, high-fidelity readout, and quantum-limited amplification. To this end, this study will also shed light on how thermodynamic constraints and field effects can be harnessed within a quantum-enhanced SD platform, thereby enabling thermal compatibility between classical and quantum workflows, isothermal all-electrical control, and on-chip scalability. This perspective is expected to play a pivotal role in the advancement of superconducting circuit-based quantum hardware with temperature-matched classical, quantum, and hybrid workflows.

AI.tldr: This study explores how superconducting diodes (SDs) can be integrated into quantum technology architecture to address control and scalability challenges in quantum hardware, focusing on nonlinearity, nonreciprocity, and quantum functionalities for on-chip circuit quantum electrodynamics (c-QED).
AI.motivation: Current quantum technology hardware faces fundamental challenges in control and scalability due to: (1) complex, bulky, and dissipative nonlinear and nonreciprocal circuit elements needed for quantum processors, and (2) temperature gradients between classical control electronics (~K) and quantum processors (~mK) creating scalability issues.
AI.method: This perspective study maps the quantum landscape of superconducting diodes onto quantum technology architecture to reveal how built-in nonlinearity, nonreciprocity, and quantum functionalities can enable on-chip integrated c-QED for scalable integration.
AI.result: The study aims to show how SDs can enable: noise-resilient qubits and qubit-interfaces, efficient power delivery, coherent control and memory, high-fidelity readout, and quantum-limited amplification. It also explores harnessing thermodynamic constraints and field effects for thermal compatibility, isothermal all-electrical control, and on-chip scalability.
AI.conclusion: This perspective is expected to play a pivotal role in advancing superconducting circuit-based quantum hardware with temperature-matched classical, quantum, and hybrid workflows.

title: First-principles study of infrared, Raman, piezoelectric and elastic properties of Mg-IV-N\textsubscript{2} (IV = Ge, Si, Sn)
summary: Mg-IV-N\textsubscript{2} compounds with IV=Si, Ge, Sn are ultra-wide band gap semiconductors with various potential electronic and optoelectronic applications. They share the \textit{Pna}2\textsubscript{1} space group crystal structure. Here we present Density Function Perturbation Theory (DFPT) calculations of the vibrational modes of these materials. We focus on the vibrational modes at the zone center to establish the relation between vibrational modes and their corresponding point-group symmetries, which determine the Raman and infrared spectra but also report the full Brillouin zone phonon dispersions and density of states. We also determine the piezoelectric tensor and the elastic compliance tensor.

AI.tldr: DFPT calculations of vibrational properties for Mg-IV-N₂ (IV=Si, Ge, Sn) ultra-wide band gap semiconductors, including zone-center modes, phonon dispersions, piezoelectric and elastic tensors.
AI.motivation: These Mg-IV-N₂ compounds are ultra-wide band gap semiconductors with potential electronic and optoelectronic applications, but their vibrational properties were not fully characterized.
AI.method: Density Function Perturbation Theory (DFPT) calculations to compute zone-center vibrational modes, full Brillouin zone phonon dispersions, density of states, piezoelectric tensor, and elastic compliance tensor.
AI.result: Established relations between vibrational modes and point-group symmetries (determining Raman/IR spectra), provided complete phonon dispersions and density of states, and determined piezoelectric and elastic tensors for all three compounds.
AI.conclusion: Provides comprehensive vibrational characterization of Mg-IV-N₂ compounds, enabling understanding of their spectroscopic properties and mechanical behavior for electronic/optoelectronic applications.

title: Lifetime and spectral function of topological heavy fermions
summary: Twisted bilayer graphene provides a paradigmatic platform for exploring the interplay between electronic topology and strong correlations. Within the topological heavy fermion model [Song and Bernevig, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 047601 (2022)], topology and electron interactions are brought together by including a weak hybridization between the bands of itinerant $c$- and localized $f$-electrons. Hybridization infuses concentrated Berry curvature into the $f$-band, while leaving it flat. These band features have motivated recent proposals of a Mott semimetal phase above the flavor-ordering temperature at charge neutrality. In this work, we develop an analytic theory of the quasiparticle dispersion and lifetime in the Mott semimetal. We reformulate the interacting flat-band Hamiltonian as an on-site Hubbard interaction defined on a set of non-orthogonal orbitals, and compute the electron Green's function using the equation-of-motion method, in close analogy with the Hubbard-III approximation. Unlike the conventional Hubbard model, in our case this approximation is controlled by a well-defined small parameter in the theory. We evaluate the electron self-energy and demonstrate the emergence of well-defined low-energy quasiparticles with the dispersion and relaxation rate proportional to the interaction strength. The quasiparticle spectrum is well-resolved in energy and in momentum down to the very vicinity of the Fermi level. Our results illustrate unconventional spectral properties arising from strong correlations and nontrivial quantum geometry, and have direct relevance for spectroscopic probes such as quantum twisting microscope experiments.

AI.tldr: This work develops an analytic theory for quasiparticle dispersion and lifetime in the Mott semimetal phase of twisted bilayer graphene. By reformulating the flat-band Hamiltonian as a Hubbard model on non-orthogonal orbitals and using the equation-of-motion method (Hubbard-III approximation), the authors show well-defined quasiparticles emerge with properties scaling with interaction strength. Results have direct relevance for spectroscopic probes like quantum twisting microscope experiments.
AI.motivation: Twisted bilayer graphene serves as a paradigmatic platform for exploring electronic topology and strong correlations. The topological heavy fermion model combines these by including weak hybridization between itinerant c- and localized f-electrons, creating concentrated Berry curvature in a flat f-band. This has motivated proposals of a Mott semimetal phase above the flavor-ordering temperature at charge neutrality, but an analytic theory of quasiparticle dispersion and lifetime in this phase was lacking.
AI.method: Reformulated the interacting flat-band Hamiltonian as an on-site Hubbard interaction on non-orthogonal orbitals; computed electron Green's function using equation-of-motion method analogous to Hubbard-III approximation, which is controlled by a well-defined small parameter in the theory.
AI.result: Evaluated electron self-energy and demonstrated emergence of well-defined low-energy quasiparticles with dispersion and relaxation rate proportional to interaction strength. The quasiparticle spectrum is well-resolved in energy and momentum down to the very vicinity of the Fermi level.
AI.conclusion: The theory establishes that low-energy quasiparticles in the Mott semimetal are well-defined with dispersion and relaxation rate proportional to interaction strength. These spectral properties are resolvable in energy and momentum even near the Fermi level, providing a framework for understanding unconventional spectral features in twisted bilayer graphene systems relevant for spectroscopic probes like quantum twisting microscopy.

title: Interlayer hybridization enables superconductivity in bilayer nickelates
summary: Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates offer a new route to high-temperature superconductivity beyond the cuprates and iron-pnictides. However, the electronic reorganization that enables superconductivity in bilayer nickelates remain unresolved, largely due to the difficulty of directly probing the superconducting phase. Here, we overcome this limitation by stabilizing superconducting (La,Pr)$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ thin films with a protective capping layer, thereby enabling direct spectroscopic access via X-ray absorption and resonant inelastic X-ray scattering. We resolve the evolution of in-plane and out-of-plane electronic states, spin and orbital excitations, and spin-density-waves across insulating, superconducting, and metallic regimes. Combining experimental results with theoretical analysis, we show that the in-plane $d_{x^2-y^2}$ states form an itinerant backbone, while superconductivity emerges only when coherent $d_{z^2}$-$p_z$-$d_{z^2}$ interlayer hybridization develops, accompanied by suppressed static spin order and strongly damped spin excitations. Oxygen stoichiometry and epitaxial strain both act on this interlayer channel, placing superconductivity within a narrow window of interlayer coherence and correlation strength. These findings identify the microscopic ingredients required for superconductivity in bilayer nickelates and provide a multiorbital picture of its emergence.

AI.tldr: This study stabilizes superconducting (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films with protective capping to enable direct spectroscopic probing, revealing that in-plane dx2-y2 states form an itinerant backbone while superconductivity emerges from coherent dz2-pz-dz2 interlayer hybridization, occurring within a narrow window of interlayer coherence and correlation strength.
AI.motivation: Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates offer a new route to high-temperature superconductivity beyond cuprates and iron-pnictides, but the electronic reorganization enabling superconductivity in bilayer nickelates remained unresolved due to difficulty in directly probing the superconducting phase.
AI.method: The researchers stabilized superconducting (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films with a protective capping layer, enabling direct spectroscopic access via X-ray absorption spectroscopy and resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) to study electronic states, spin and orbital excitations across insulating, superconducting, and metallic regimes.
AI.result: The study resolved the evolution of in-plane and out-of-plane electronic states, spin and orbital excitations, and spin-density-waves. They found that in-plane dx2-y2 states form an itinerant backbone, while superconductivity emerges only when coherent dz2-pz-dz2 interlayer hybridization develops, accompanied by suppressed static spin order and strongly damped spin excitations. Both oxygen stoichiometry and epitaxial strain act on this interlayer channel, placing superconductivity within a narrow window of interlayer coherence and correlation strength.
AI.conclusion: These findings identify the microscopic ingredients required for superconductivity in bilayer nickelates and provide a multiorbital picture of its emergence, offering crucial insights for understanding and developing high-temperature superconductivity in nickelate systems.

title: Anomalous Platinum and Oxygen Transport during Electroforming of NbOx Memristors
summary: Electroforming of metal-oxide-metal memristors is generally attributed to the creation of oxygen-vacancy filaments within the oxide, with noble metal electrodes such as Pt and Au remaining chemically inert. Here, we demonstrate that electroforming and subsequent operation of Pt/NbOx/Nb2O5/Pt devices can induce an unexpected and highly correlated redistribution of both oxygen and platinum. Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry reveals a filamentary pathway characterized by micrometer-scale oxygen enrichment extending from the Nb2O5 layer through NbOx and deep into the Pt top electrode. Surprisingly, this is accompanied by the formation of a Pt-rich filament penetrating the oxide stack along the same filamentary path. Finite-element and lumped-element modelling show that current-controlled negative-differential-resistance operation produces localized Joule heating and high-frequency thermal cycling, which strongly enhances oxygen migration and enables thermally assisted Pt diffusion along vacancy-rich pathways. These findings reveal a previously unrecognized metal-ion transport mechanism in NbOx memristors and highlight the critical role of post-forming electrical dynamics in determining filament chemistry, stability, and device reliability.

AI.tldr: Electroforming in Pt/NbOx/Nb2O5/Pt memristors induces unexpected correlated redistribution of oxygen and platinum, forming a Pt-rich filament along oxygen-enriched pathways due to Joule heating and thermal cycling effects.
AI.motivation: Previously, electroforming was attributed to oxygen-vacancy filament creation with chemically inert noble metal electrodes. This study challenges that assumption by demonstrating that Pt electrodes can actively participate in filament formation.
AI.method: Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) was used to characterize the filamentary pathway, complemented by finite-element and lumped-element modelling to understand the thermal dynamics.
AI.result: Micrometer-scale oxygen enrichment was observed extending from Nb2O5 through NbOx deep into the Pt top electrode, accompanied by Pt-rich filament formation along the same path. Current-controlled negative-differential-resistance operation produces localized Joule heating and high-frequency thermal cycling that enables thermally assisted Pt diffusion along vacancy-rich pathways.
AI.conclusion: This work reveals a previously unrecognized metal-ion transport mechanism in NbOx memristors and highlights the critical role of post-forming electrical dynamics in determining filament chemistry, stability, and device reliability.

title: Two pathways to break the insulating state in a correlated transition metal oxide
summary: Correlated transition metal oxides present exciting prospects as switches or memory and storage devices owing to the possibility to control electronic properties using various external stimuli. While their complex behaviour is known to stem from interplay between electronic correlations, atomic structure and orbital physics, they remain poorly understood on the microscopic level. Here, we investigate such origins as a function of temperature and pressure in the transition metal oxide Ti3O5. We find that the insulating room-temperature phase is characterized by one-dimensional zig-zag chains composed by two types of titanium dimers forming orbital selective valence bonds. At the thermal phase transition, one type of titanium dimer breaks up, resulting in an insulator to metal transition with a large orbital repopulation between the two states. Moreover, optical spectroscopy reveals that an additional pressure-driven insulator to metal transition occurs in Ti3O5 at room temperature. The phenomenology of this novel pressure-induced metallic transition is completely different from the insofar studied transitions and results from a competition between intra- and inter-dimer hopping. Our combined results suggest that Ti3O5 is a prototypical correlated transition metal oxide, where both correlations as well as orbital interactions need to be considered to fully understand the evolution of the electronic states.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the microscopic origins of electronic properties in Ti3O5 under temperature and pressure, revealing that the insulating phase features one-dimensional zig-zag chains with orbital selective valence bonds, and discovering a novel pressure-driven insulator-to-metal transition distinct from the thermal transition.
AI.motivation: Correlated transition metal oxides are promising for switches and memory devices due to controllable electronic properties, but their complex behavior stemming from electronic correlations, atomic structure, and orbital physics remains poorly understood at the microscopic level.
AI.method: Investigation of Ti3O5 as a function of temperature and pressure using experimental techniques including optical spectroscopy to study the electronic structure and phase transitions.
AI.result: The insulating room-temperature phase shows one-dimensional zig-zag chains with two types of titanium dimers forming orbital selective valence bonds. At the thermal phase transition, one dimer type breaks up, causing an insulator-to-metal transition with orbital repopulation. Additionally, a pressure-driven insulator-to-metal transition occurs at room temperature, arising from competition between intra- and inter-dimer hopping.
AI.conclusion: Ti3O5 serves as a prototypical correlated transition metal oxide where both electronic correlations and orbital interactions must be considered to fully understand the evolution of electronic states, offering insights into controlling electronic properties for device applications.

title: Fermi-liquid versus non-Fermi-liquid/'strange-metal' fits to the electrical resistivity in the quantum critical magnetic regime of an unconventional superconductor
summary: The question of a possible quantum critical point lying inside of a superconducting phase is central for understanding unconventional superconductivity. In various unconventional superconductors, non-Fermi-liquid/'strange-metal' $T^{n}$ variations, with $n<2$, of the electrical resistivity have been identified as the signature of magnetic quantum criticality. However, a difficulty is to prove experimentally that a non-Fermi-liquid/'strange-metal' law identified at temperatures above the superconducting temperature is the signature of an intrinsic zero-temperature quantum critical regime. In the heavy-fermion paramagnet UTe$_2$, unconventional superconductivity develops in the vicinity of a metamagnetic quantum phase transition induced by a magnetic field, and the quantum critical magnetic properties are suspected to play a role for the superconducting mechanism. In this work, we present a comparative analysis of electrical resistivity data collected on two UTe$_2$ samples of different qualities, in magnetic fields tilted by angles $θ\simeq35-40$~$^\circ$ from $\mathbf{b}$ to $\mathbf{c}$. Fits to the data have been performed either with a Fermi-liquid function $ρ=ρ_0+AT^{2}$ or with a non-Fermi-liquid/'strange-metal' function $ρ=ρ_0+A_nT^n$. Near to a superconducting phase induced beyond 40~T, non-physical residual resistivities $ρ_0<0$ are extracted from the $T^n$ fits, revealing that a 'hidden' Fermi-liquid $T^2$ regime may be ultimately recovered at low temperature. The results obtained here highlight the importance to investigate high-quality samples with low residual resistivity to confirm - or not - the presence of a suspected 'hidden' quantum critical behavior masked by superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This study investigates quantum criticality in heavy-fermion superconductor UTe2 through electrical resistivity measurements. The discovery of negative residual resistivities from non-Fermi-liquid fits suggests a 'hidden' Fermi-liquid regime exists at low temperatures, masked by superconductivity. The work emphasizes the need for high-quality samples with low residual resistivity to confirm suspected quantum critical behavior.
AI.motivation: The challenge in proving that non-Fermi-liquid/strange-metal behavior observed above superconducting temperature is truly indicative of an intrinsic zero-temperature quantum critical regime. In UTe2, unconventional superconductivity develops near a metamagnetic quantum phase transition induced by magnetic field, and quantum critical magnetic properties are suspected to play a role in the superconducting mechanism.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of electrical resistivity data on two UTe2 samples of different quality, measured in magnetic fields tilted at angles θ≈35-40° from b to c axis. Data fitted with both Fermi-liquid (ρ=ρ0+AT²) and non-Fermi-liquid/strange-metal (ρ=ρ0+AT^n) functions.
AI.result: Near the superconducting phase induced beyond 40 T, non-physical residual resistivities (ρ0<0) are extracted from T^n fits, revealing that a 'hidden' Fermi-liquid T² regime may be recovered at low temperature. Higher quality samples yield better results for revealing the underlying quantum critical behavior.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that near a superconducting phase induced beyond 40 T, non-physical negative residual resistivities extracted from T^n fits suggest a 'hidden' Fermi-liquid T^2 regime may exist at low temperatures. The findings emphasize the importance of investigating high-quality samples with low residual resistivity to confirm or refute the presence of suspected quantum critical behavior masked by superconductivity. This provides evidence for quantum criticality in UTe2 but highlights the experimental challenges in confirming such behavior when superconductivity intervenes.

title: Morphological Transition: From Meanders to Mound Structures
summary: Mound formation on flat and miscut crystal surfaces exhibits distinct growth behaviors. While mound structures are the predominant feature on flat surfaces, miscut surfaces display a smooth transition from meandered patterns to three-dimensional mounds, depending on both internal and external conditions. We investigate this morphological evolution-from meander-like surface patterns to faceted pyramidal structures-using a vicinal Cellular Automata modeling framework. The transition is shown to be governed by the competition between the Ehrlich-Schwoebel barrier and adatom mobility on terraces. Under moderate barrier strengths and sufficiently high terrace diffusivity, the system demonstrates a reversible transition from mounded configurations to regular step meandered patterns. This reveals a complex interplay between kinetic barriers and mass transport. Our simulations cover a wide range of growth conditions, including variations in deposition flux, surface diffusion rates, temperature, and miscut angle. By applying the height-height correlation function, we calculate the correlation lengths along and across the steps and analyze their scaling behavior. These results offer insight into the continuum pathways that connect distinct classes of surface structures and provide a unified framework for describing pattern evolution across different crystal growth regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates mound formation and morphological evolution on crystal surfaces using Cellular Automata modeling, revealing conditions under which surfaces transition between meandered patterns and 3D pyramidal structures.
AI.motivation: Understanding morphological evolution on crystal surfaces is crucial for controlling thin film growth and surface patterning in material science. The distinct behaviors between flat and miscut surfaces require a unified framework to predict and control surface structures under various growth conditions.
AI.method: The study employs a vicinal Cellular Automata modeling framework to simulate surface growth. It investigates the competition between Ehrlich-Schwoebel barrier and adatom mobility across a wide range of conditions including deposition flux, surface diffusion rates, temperature, and miscut angle. Analysis uses height-height correlation function to calculate correlation lengths and scaling behavior.
AI.result: The research demonstrates that morphological evolution from meander-like patterns to faceted pyramidal structures is governed by the competition between Ehrlich-Schwoebel barrier and adatom mobility. Under moderate barrier strengths and high terrace diffusivity, the system exhibits a reversible transition between mounded and regular step meandered patterns. This reveals a complex interplay between kinetic barriers and mass transport.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a unified framework for describing pattern evolution across different crystal growth regimes and offer insight into continuum pathways connecting distinct classes of surface structures. Understanding these transitions enables better control of thin film morphology in practical crystal growth applications.

title: The Two Orbital, Interacting Hatano-Nelson Model
summary: The single orbital, one-dimensional, Hatano-Nelson Hamiltonian provides deep insight into the physics of non-Hermiticity, resulting from asymmetric left/right hopping, and its connections to localization. In the absence of disorder, its single particle eigenvalues $E_α$ lie on an ellipse in the complex plane whose extent in the imaginary direction is controlled by the degree of asymmetry. When randomness is introduced, two sets of real eigenvalues emerge at the extremes of the largest and smallest real part of $E_α$. These real eigenvalues are associated with localized eigenvectors. For spinless fermions, increasing near-neighbor interactions first cause a transition to a charge density wave phase, and ultimately, on finite lattices, a collapse of all eigenvalues to the real axis. In this paper, we explore the presence of real eigenvalues in the interacting, two-particle sector for the spinful case (Hubbard model) in a two-chain (two-band) geometry with a Hermitian interchain hopping. Our key results are to obtain the ``phase" diagrams for the existence of a purely real spectrum, as a function of the interaction strength, degree of non-Hermiticity, and interchain hopping. We study the sensitivity to boundary conditions of the spectral properties of our two-chain model with winding number analysis and explore the relationship between PBC doublon states and OBC skin modes. To address the question of stability in such non-equilibrium systems, we solve the dynamics at low filling according to Lindbladian evolution and find that the non-Hermitian description is able to qualitatively describe such systems.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Hatano-Nelson non-Hermitian model to interacting spinful fermions in a two-chain Hubbard geometry, mapping out phase diagrams for real eigenvalue existence and studying boundary condition effects and Lindbladian dynamics.
AI.motivation: The Hatano-Nelson model provides insights into non-Hermiticity and localization. Prior work showed real eigenvalues emerge with disorder in single-particle case and with interactions in spinless fermion case. This work aims to explore real eigenvalues in the interacting two-particle sector for the spinful Hubbard model in a two-chain geometry, addressing questions about spectral properties and stability in non-equilibrium systems.
AI.method: The authors study a two-chain (two-band) Hatano-Nelson-Hubbard model with Hermitian interchain hopping. They construct phase diagrams varying interaction strength, non-Hermiticity degree, and interchain hopping. They analyze boundary condition sensitivity using winding number analysis, explore relationship between periodic boundary conditions (PBC) doublon states and open boundary conditions (OBC) skin modes, and solve Lindbladian dynamics at low filling.
AI.result: The key results include phase diagrams showing existence regions of purely real spectrum as functions of interaction strength, non-Hermiticity, and interchain hopping. The study reveals sensitivity to boundary conditions and finds connections between PBC doublon states and OBC skin modes. The non-Hermitian description qualitatively describes the Lindbladian dynamics at low filling.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that non-Hermitian formalism can qualitatively describe interacting spinful fermion systems in two-chain geometries, establishing boundary condition-dependent spectral properties and providing phase diagrams for real spectrum existence. This contributes to understanding non-equilibrium physics in non-Hermitian many-body systems.

title: Type II Lifshitz invariant and optically active Higgs mode in time-reversal symmetry broken superconductors
summary: Lifshitz invariant is a symmetry-allowed term in the Ginzburg-Landau free energy of an ordered phase, involving the order parameters and a single spatial derivative, which serves as a source of unusual optical responses. Here we introduce a ``type II" Lifshitz invariant for superconductors, which changes its sign under the particle-hole transformation and can be distinguished from the ordinary particle-hole even ``type I" Lifshitz invariant. We show that the type II Lifshitz invariant appears only in superconductors that break time-reversal symmetry and allows the Higgs mode to be visible in the optical conductivity spectrum. We provide a classification of all pairs of irreducible corepresentations of order parameters in the magnetic point groups that admit a type II Lifshitz invariant. We also numerically calculate the optical conductivity for various models of time-reversal symmetry broken multiband superconductors, finding agreement with the group-theoretical analysis. Our results establish a universal class of time-reversal symmetry broken superconductors hosting an optically active Higgs mode.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a "type II" Lifshitz invariant in superconductors that changes sign under particle-hole transformation, distinguishing it from the conventional particle-hole even "type I" variant. This type II invariant appears exclusively in superconductors that break time-reversal symmetry and enables the Higgs mode to be observable in optical conductivity spectra.
AI.motivation: The Lifshitz invariant is a symmetry-allowed term in Ginzburg-Landau free energy involving order parameters with a single spatial derivative, known to cause unusual optical responses. However, the distinction between different types of Lifshitz invariants and their implications for observable phenomena like Higgs mode visibility remained unclear. This work aims to clarify this classification and its physical consequences.
AI.method: The authors performed group-theoretical analysis using irreducible corepresentations of magnetic point groups to classify all order parameter pairs that admit a type II Lifshitz invariant. They supplemented this theoretical analysis with numerical calculations of optical conductivity for various models of time-reversal symmetry broken multiband superconductors.
AI.result: The type II Lifshitz invariant appears only in superconductors breaking time-reversal symmetry and allows the Higgs mode to become visible in the optical conductivity spectrum. The group-theoretical analysis provides a complete classification of compatible order parameter pairs in magnetic point groups, and numerical calculations on multiband superconductors confirm these predictions.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a universal class of time-reversal symmetry broken superconductors that host an optically active Higgs mode, bridging symmetry analysis with observable spectroscopic features. The distinction between type I and type II Lifshitz invariants provides a systematic framework for identifying superconductors where Higgs mode can be detected via optical conductivity measurements.

title: Nonmagnetic-magnetic Transitions in Rutile RuO2
summary: Rutile RuO$_2$ has attracted great interest recently, as its magnetic ground state remains controversial. Experimental studies have reported either nonmagnetic or altermagnetic (AM) ground states in different crystalline samples of RuO$_2$, highlighting the need for a reasonable explanation to resolve this contradiction. In this study, density functional theory calculations are performed to reveal the correlation-sensitive and strain-dependent magnetism of bulk RuO$_2$. On one hand, multiple AM phases with different magnitudes of the spin magnetic moment are identified in the Hubbard parameter space for RuO$_2$. On the other hand, when appropriate strains which significantly change the crystal cell volume are applied, the ground state of RuO$_2$ can undergo transitions between the nonmagnetic state with no spin splitting and the magnetic states with spin splitting in the band structure. These findings not only demonstrate intriguing physics in 4d-electron-correlated RuO$_2$, but also retain its potential for spintronic applications.

AI.tldr: This study uses DFT calculations to investigate the controversial magnetic ground state of RuO2, discovering that both correlation effects (via Hubbard parameters) and applied strain can induce transitions between nonmagnetic and altermagnetic states.
AI.motivation: The magnetic ground state of RuO2 remains controversial, with experimental studies reporting conflicting results - either nonmagnetic or altermagnetic (AM) ground states in different crystalline samples. This contradiction needed a reasonable explanation.
AI.method: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to investigate the correlation-sensitive and strain-dependent magnetism of bulk RuO2, exploring the Hubbard parameter space and applying strain that significantly changes the crystal cell volume.
AI.result: Multiple altermagnetic phases with different magnitudes of spin magnetic moments were identified in the Hubbard parameter space. Applied strains that significantly change the crystal cell volume can induce transitions between nonmagnetic states (no spin splitting) and magnetic states (with spin splitting in the band structure).
AI.conclusion: These findings demonstrate intriguing physics in 4d-electron-correlated RuO2 and retain its potential for spintronic applications, providing a framework for understanding the contradictory experimental observations.

title: Discovery of an odd-parity f-wave charge order in a kagome metal
summary: The spontaneous breaking of symmetries is a cornerstone of physics, defining the phases of matter from the cosmological scale to the quantum realm. In condensed matter, electronic orders are classified by their behavior under fundamental symmetries like spatial inversion (parity). While even-parity orders, such as conventional superconductivity and charge density waves, are ubiquitous, their odd-parity counterparts--predicted to host exotic phenomena such as gapless quasiparticle excitations and novel collective modes--are comparatively elusive states of quantum matter. Here, using high-resolution scanning tunneling microscopy and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy on the kagome metal CsV$_3$Sb$_5$, we report the discovery of an inversion symmetry-breaking $f$-wave charge bond order. We show that this phase, which preserves translation symmetry, is stabilized by the spontaneous opening of a spectral gap at a previously overlooked Dirac point, providing a textbook condensed-matter realization of the Gross-Neveu model for dynamical mass generation and parity breaking. Intriguingly, this $f$-wave order is itself a intervening phase, vanishing abruptly below a temperature of 10\,K and pointing to a subsequent transition into a `hidden' electronic state that is invisible to local STM probes. Our findings establish odd-parity charge order as a novel phase of matter, here, embedded within the intricate hierarchy of correlated electronic orders on the kagome lattice.

AI.tldr: Researchers discovered an odd-parity f-wave charge bond order in kagome metal CsV3Sb5 using STM and ARPES. This inversion symmetry-breaking phase preserves translation symmetry and opens a spectral gap at a Dirac point, realizing the Gross-Neveu model. The order disappears below 10K, revealing a hidden electronic state.
AI.motivation: Odd-parity electronic orders (predicted to host exotic phenomena like gapless quasiparticle excitations and novel collective modes) are comparatively elusive compared to even-parity orders. This study sought to discover such odd-parity charge order in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5.
AI.method: High-resolution scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES)
AI.result: Discovery of an inversion symmetry-breaking f-wave charge bond order that preserves translation symmetry. This phase is stabilized by opening a spectral gap at a previously overlooked Dirac point, providing a condensed-matter realization of the Gross-Neveu model for dynamical mass generation and parity breaking. The f-wave order vanishes below 10K, pointing to a subsequent "hidden" electronic state invisible to local STM probes.
AI.conclusion: The discovery establishes odd-parity charge order as a novel phase of matter embedded within the hierarchy of correlated electronic orders on the kagome lattice. The f-wave order acts as an intervening phase that vanishes below 10K, revealing a subsequent "hidden" electronic state invisible to local STM probes.

title: Quantum fluctuations and the emergence of in-gap Higgs mode in superconductors
summary: We extend the well-established action of the Higgs mode in $s$-wave superconductors to include quantum fluctuations (QFs). We find that already one-loop quantum corrections to the Higgs propagator shift its eigenfrequency below the superconducting energy gap $2Δ$. Consequently, the Higgs mode appears as an undamped pole below the quasiparticle continuum, leading to drastically sharper experimental signatures. We demonstrate this by calculating two characteristic fingerprints of the Higgs mode, namely in Third Harmonic Generation (THG) and inelastic Raman scattering signals. More generally, gaps measured in $s$-wave superconductors with different experimental techniques (such as scanning tunneling microscope and Raman scattering) may be different due to fluctuation corrections. Since already arbitrarily weak QFs lead to the shift and to the new pole, our results shed some light on other amplitude modes even for systems with weak QFs, including charge density waves, (anti-) ferromagnets, or cold atom fermionic condensates.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that quantum fluctuations in s-wave superconductors shift the Higgs mode below the energy gap 2Δ, making it appear as a sharp, detectable pole rather than being buried in the quasiparticle continuum. This explains the observed sharp experimental signatures and has implications for amplitude modes in other quantum systems.
AI.motivation: The Higgs amplitude mode in s-wave superconductors is typically hidden within the quasiparticle continuum, making it difficult to observe experimentally. This work aims to understand why the Higgs mode shows sharper experimental signatures than expected from standard theory, and whether quantum fluctuations play a key role.
AI.method: The authors calculate one-loop quantum corrections to the Higgs propagator, then compute Third Harmonic Generation (THG) and inelastic Raman scattering signals as fingerprints of the Higgs mode. They analyze how these spectroscopic measurements are affected by the fluctuation-induced shift in the Higgs eigenfrequency.
AI.result: One-loop quantum corrections shift the Higgs eigenfrequency below the superconducting gap 2Δ, creating an undamped pole below the quasiparticle continuum. This leads to drastically sharper signatures in THG and inelastic Raman scattering. The authors also note that different experimental techniques may measure different gap values due to these fluctuation corrections.
AI.conclusion: This work reveals that quantum fluctuations cause the Higgs mode to appear as a sharper, below-gap pole in s-wave superconductors, providing a resolution to the long-standing question of why the Higgs mode is hard to detect experimentally. The findings have broad implications for understanding amplitude modes in other quantum systems with weak quantum fluctuations.

title: Spin-Valley-Mismatched Altermagnet for Giant Tunneling Magnetoresistance
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures. npj Computational Materials, in press
summary: Altermagnet-based heterojunctions have demonstrated magnetoresistive effects in experiments, however, a predictive theoretical model for non-ferromagnetic structures has remained elusive. In this work, we develop a tunneling-based spin-transport theory that explicitly incorporates the transverse-wavevector ($\bf{k}_\|$)-dependent spin polarization of an altermagnet's transport channels, enabling the prediction of giant tunneling magnetoresistance (TMR). Based on the theory, we predict that the altermagnet KV$_2$Se$_2$O can reach the extreme limit of magnetoresistance. By performing first-principles transport calculations, we verify that magnetic tunnel junctions using the metallic KV$_2$Se$_2$O as the electrodes and few-layer MgO as the spacer exhibit zero-bias magnetoresistance larger than $7.57\times10^7$\%, which is robust against the bias and thickness of the spacer. Our research provides a quantitative design principle for next-generation spin-electronic devices and establishes KV$_2$Se$_2$O/MgO/KV$_2$Se$_2$O as a leading candidate material system for room-temperature ultra-high-density non-volatile memory.

AI.tldr: Development of a tunneling-based spin-transport theory for altermagnets that predicts giant tunneling magnetoresistance (TMR), with KV2Se2O/MgO/KV2Se2O magnetic tunnel junctions achieving magnetoresistance over 7.57×10^7%.
AI.motivation: Altermagnet-based heterojunctions show magnetoresistive effects experimentally, but a predictive theoretical model for non-ferromagnetic structures has been lacking.
AI.method: Developed a k_-dependent spin-transport theory incorporating transverse-wavevector-dependent spin polarization of altermagnet transport channels; performed first-principles transport calculations.
AI.result: KV2Se2O/MgO/KV2Se2O magnetic tunnel junctions exhibit zero-bias magnetoresistance >7.57×10^7%, robust against bias and spacer thickness.
AI.conclusion: Provides quantitative design principle for next-generation spin-electronic devices; establishes KV2Se2O/MgO/KV2Se2O as leading candidate for room-temperature ultra-high-density non-volatile memory.

title: Level statistics of the disordered Haldane-Shastry model with $1/r^α$ interaction
summary: Understanding how the interaction range and various types of disorder affect the level statistics of many-body quantum systems and lead to the emergence of many-body localization (MBL) is a challenging open frontier. We study the level statistics of a variant of the spin-$1/2$ Haldane-Shastry model with $1/r^α$ interactions, where $α{\geq}0$ parametrizes the range of the interactions, in the presence of position disorder and/or random magnetic fields. We find that neither position disorder nor random magnetic fields alone yields pristine Poisson statistics in this long-range interacting system; however, Poisson statistics emerge in their combined presence, suggesting the emergence of MBL when both types of disorder coexist. Interestingly, once random magnetic fields break the $SU(2)$ symmetry, the strength of the position disorder, $δ$, appears to play an important role, as evidenced by an approximate scaling collapse of the disorder-averaged gap ratios that is parametrized in terms of a single parameter, $αδ$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies level statistics in a long-range interacting spin-1/2 Haldane-Shastry model with 1/r^α interactions under position disorder and random magnetic fields, revealing that MBL emerges only when both types of disorder coexist.
AI.motivation: Understanding how interaction range and various types of disorder affect level statistics and lead to many-body localization (MBL) in quantum many-body systems remains an open challenge.
AI.method: The authors study the level statistics of a variant of the spin-1/2 Haldane-Shastry model with 1/r^α interactions (where α ≥ 0 parametrizes interaction range) in the presence of position disorder and/or random magnetic fields.
AI.result: Neither position disorder nor random magnetic fields alone yields pristine Poisson statistics in this long-range interacting system; however, Poisson statistics emerge when both types of disorder coexist, indicating MBL emergence. Once random magnetic fields break the SU(2) symmetry, the position disorder strength δ plays an important role, as shown by an approximate scaling collapse of disorder-averaged gap ratios parameterized by αδ.
AI.conclusion: MBL emerges only when both position disorder and random magnetic fields are present simultaneously in the long-range interacting Haldane-Shastry model, and a universal scaling behavior governed by αδ characterizes the transition.

title: Abrikosov vortices in altermagnetic superconductors
summary: We study the penetration of an external magnetic field into a superconductor with collinear $d$-wave altermagnetic order. We demonstrate that instead of circular Abrikosov vortices, the magnetic field generates elliptical vortices with their major axis oriented along one of the crystallographic axis, along which the altermagnetic spin splitting is maximal. Upon reversing the component of the magnetic field parallel to the altermagnetic Néel vector, the vortices reorient towards the other crystallographic axis with maximal spin splitting. We demonstrate that this effect originates from an altermagnetism-induced anisotropy of the effective mass, which is controlled by the coupling between the external magnetic field and the Néel vector. As a consequence, a superconducting film hosting such altermagnetic order and containing pinning defects exhibits nonreciprocal magnetization curves under reversal of the magnetic field parallel to its Néel vector, due to the different vortex--vortex interaction energies for the two field orientations. Our results broaden the understanding of the coexistence of altermagnetism and superconductivity, both in materials hosting these orders intrinsically or in superconductor/altermagnet hybrid structures, and open new experimental avenues for exploring supercurrent vortices in these systems.

AI.tldr: Study of magnetic field penetration into superconductors with collinear d-wave altermagnetic order, revealing elliptical vortices instead of circular Abrikosov vortices, with orientation controlled by the Néel vector.
AI.motivation: Investigate the coexistence of altermagnetism and superconductivity, and understand how magnetic fields behave in such hybrid systems where both orders can coexist or be engineered in heterostructures.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of magnetic field penetration and vortex formation in superconductors with altermagnetic order, considering anisotropy of effective mass induced by altermagnetism.
AI.result: Discovery of elliptical vortices with major axis along crystallographic axis of maximal altermagnetic spin splitting; vortices reorient when the magnetic field component parallel to the Néel vector is reversed; nonreciprocal magnetization in films with pinning defects.
AI.conclusion: Altermagnetism induces effective mass anisotropy that controls vortex orientation. This provides a mechanism for nonreciprocal magnetic response and opens experimental avenues for studying vortices in altermagnet/superconductor systems, relevant for both intrinsic coexistence and engineered heterostructures.

title: Pattern formation during melting of lamellar eutectics
summary: We present a study of the melting dynamics of a two-phase eutectic solid. In situ, thin-sample experiments using a transparent eutectic alloy and two-dimensional phase field simulations calibrated for the very same alloy are combined to assess pattern formation during directional melting in a temperature gradient. Depending on the melting velocity $V_m$ and the spacing $λ$ of the pre-solidified lamellar microstructure, an unexpectedly rich diversity of melting patterns is observed, with good agreement between experiments and simulations. We unravel the different physical mechanisms leading to this diversity, and establish the scaling behaviors of (i) the penetration of the liquid along the solid-solid interface at large $V_m$, (ii) the thickening of the primary-phase fingers at low $V_m$, and (iii) a period-doubling instability for small $λ$ values. Our study provides a fundamental basis for further investigations of eutectic melting, including additive manufacturing during which melting/solidification cycles take place.

AI.tldr: This study combines experiments and simulations to investigate melting dynamics in eutectic solids, revealing diverse pattern formation mechanisms during directional melting that depend on velocity and microstructure spacing, with applications to additive manufacturing.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to understand pattern formation during directional melting in a two-phase eutectic solid, as this has implications for additive manufacturing processes involving melting/solidification cycles.
AI.method: The approach combined in situ thin-sample experiments using a transparent eutectic alloy with two-dimensional phase field simulations calibrated for the same alloy to study directional melting in a temperature gradient.
AI.result: The study observed unexpectedly rich diversity of melting patterns depending on melting velocity and lamellar spacing, with good agreement between experiments and simulations. Different physical mechanisms were identified for the observed pattern diversity.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes scaling behaviors for three distinct melting phenomena: (i) liquid penetration along solid-solid interfaces at high melting velocities, (ii) primary-phase finger thickening at low velocities, and (iii) period-doubling instabilities at small lamellar spacings. This provides a fundamental basis for further eutectic melting investigations, particularly in additive manufacturing applications involving repeated melting/solidification cycles.

title: Nontrivial three-sublattice magnetization in the easy-axis spin-1/2 XXZ antiferromagnet on the triangular lattice
summary: We investigate the ground-state magnetic structure of the spin-$1/2$ XXZ antiferromagnet on the triangular lattice in the easy-axis regime using the density-matrix renormalization group. By applying spiral boundary conditions, we exactly map finite $L\times L$ clusters onto one-dimensional chains while avoiding the spatial anisotropy inherent in cylindrical geometries. From symmetry-broken local magnetization profiles, we extract the three-sublattice moments and track their evolution with anisotropy. At the isotropic point, we obtain a positive sublattice moment of $0.21671$, consistent with previous numerical estimates. In the easy-axis regime, the ordered moments remain close to a zero-magnetization three-sublattice structure of the form $(2m,-m,-m)$ over a broad range of $Δ$. Extrapolation in $1/Δ$ shows that the positive sublattice moment stays well below the classical saturation value $1/2$, approaching $0.41873$ as $Δ\to\infty$, while the magnitude of the negative sublattice moment approaches $0.20832$. We further compare the energies of the Y state and the up-down-down state and find that the Y state is favored at zero field. Independent thermodynamic-limit energy calculations, performed without assuming any particular ordered pattern, yield an energy consistent with the Y-state solution. These results show that the easy-axis ground state does not simply cross over to a trivially saturated collinear Ising state, but instead remains a nontrivial three-sublattice ordered state selected from the macroscopically degenerate Ising manifold by quantum fluctuations.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the ground-state magnetic structure of the spin-1/2 XXZ antiferromagnet on the triangular lattice in the easy-axis regime using DMRG. It finds that the ground state remains a nontrivial three-sublattice ordered state (Y state) rather than simply crossing over to a saturated collinear Ising state, with quantum fluctuations selecting this structure from the degenerate Ising manifold.
AI.motivation: Understanding the ground state of frustrated quantum magnets is important for uncovering novel quantum phases and phase transitions. The triangular lattice XXZ antiferromagnet is a prototypical system where competition between geometric frustration and quantum fluctuations leads to nontrivial ordered states. The easy-axis regime (Δ > 1) is particularly interesting because the classical ground state becomes highly degenerate, and quantum fluctuations are expected to play a crucial role in selecting the actual ordered structure.
AI.method: The authors use density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) with spiral boundary conditions to map finite L×L clusters onto one-dimensional chains, avoiding spatial anisotropy inherent in cylindrical geometries. They extract three-sublattice moments from symmetry-broken local magnetization profiles and perform thermodynamic-limit energy calculations without assuming any particular ordered pattern.
AI.result: At the isotropic point (Δ=1), the positive sublattice moment is 0.21671, consistent with previous estimates. In the easy-axis regime, ordered moments remain close to a (2m, -m, -m) three-sublattice structure. Extrapolation in 1/Δ shows the positive sublattice moment approaches 0.41873 as Δ→∞ (well below classical saturation 1/2), while negative sublattice moment approaches 0.20832. The Y state is found to be favored over the up-down-down state at zero field, with thermodynamic-limit energy calculations consistent with the Y-state solution.
AI.conclusion: The easy-axis ground state does not simply cross over to a trivially saturated collinear Ising state. Instead, it remains a nontrivial three-sublattice ordered state selected from the macroscopically degenerate Ising manifold by quantum fluctuations. This demonstrates the importance of quantum fluctuations in determining the ground state of frustrated magnets even in the strong anisotropy limit.

title: Discovering structural, electronic and excitonic properties of bulk, nanostructured and doped C3N4 in diamond- and graphitic-like phases
comment: 41 pages, 11 figures, submitted to The Journal of Physical Chemistry
summary: In this systematic density functional theory study, we compare a standard gradient corrected functional (PBE) with a long-range hybrid functional (HSE06), with and without correction for the dispersion forces, relatively to their ability to correctly reproduce structural and electronic properties of different bulk 3D C3N4 phases, encompassing diamond- and graphitic-like models. Corrugation is found to provide further stabilization to the layered structures with all methods. We observe that HSE06-D3 method provides results in good agreement with experimental data and with more sophisticated G0W0 calculations. Based on that, we exploited the method to investigate the nature of the bulk triplet excitons in these C3N4 structures to evaluate the S0-T1 energy difference, the selftrapping triplet exciton energy and the photoluminescence emission energy, since this is a promising vis-light photocatalyst. Nanostructuring (0D and 2D) is another relevant aspect of these materials in practical applications, therefore we have considered the effect of single or multilayer exfoliation or space confinement in nanoparticles. Finally, we also discuss how the introduction of extrinsic dopants (e.g. S atoms) in the nanostructures modifies the atomic and electronic structure.

AI.tldr: DFT benchmark study comparing PBE and HSE06 functionals with dispersion corrections for C3N4 materials. HSE06-D3 is recommended and used to investigate triplet excitons, nanostructuring effects, and S-doping for vis-light photocatalysis applications.
AI.motivation: To identify appropriate computational methods for correctly reproducing structural and electronic properties of bulk 3D C3N4 phases (diamond- and graphitic-like) for photocatalytic applications
AI.method: Systematic DFT study comparing PBE, HSE06 with/without D3 dispersion correction; validated against G0W0 calculations; applied to analyze triplet excitons, nanostructures (0D/2D), and S-doping effects
AI.result: HSE06-D3 provides results in good agreement with experimental data and G0W0 calculations. Corrugation stabilizes layered structures. Triplet exciton properties and effects of nanostructuring/extrinsic dopants were successfully characterized.
AI.conclusion: HSE06-D3 is recommended for studying C3N4 materials. The method successfully reveals bulk triplet exciton properties (S0-T1 energy difference, self-trapping energy, photoluminescence emission). Nanostructuring and S-doping significantly modify atomic and electronic structures, making these promising vis-light photocatalysts.

title: Unconventional plasmon dynamics due to strong correlations in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$
summary: Plasmon modes, their dispersion, and the onset of damping when approaching the electron-hole continuum are well understood when electron correlations are weak. However, we know little about how this picture is modified and what additional features emerge in strongly correlated materials. Here, we present a fully ab initio approach to plasmon excitations that combines density functional theory with dynamical mean-field theory, and we use it to reconcile controversial electron energy-loss spectroscopy results in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$. In particular, we show that electronic correlations reproduce the plasmon dispersion, while generating a large intrinsic width already below the electron-hole continuum. An additional high-energy peak reflecting transitions between incoherent features and a sharp increase of the plasmon's energy-momentum dispersion, akin to waterfalls in photoemission spectroscopy, are identified as genuine correlation effects.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an ab initio approach combining density functional theory with dynamical mean-field theory to study plasmon excitations in strongly correlated materials, resolving controversial electron energy-loss spectroscopy results in Sr2RuO4.
AI.motivation: While plasmon modes in weakly correlated electrons are well understood, it remains unclear how this picture changes in strongly correlated materials. The study aims to reconcile controversial experimental results and understand correlation effects.
AI.method: The authors developed a fully ab initio approach combining density functional theory (DFT) with dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to investigate plasmon excitations.
AI.result: The study found that: (1) electronic correlations reproduce the plasmon dispersion; (2) correlations generate large intrinsic width below the electron-hole continuum; (3) a high-energy peak reflecting transitions between incoherent features emerges; (4) a sharp increase in the plasmon's energy-momentum dispersion (akin to waterfalls in photoemission spectroscopy) is observed.
AI.conclusion: The high-energy peak and waterfall-like dispersion are identified as genuine correlation effects unique to strongly correlated materials, going beyond the conventional understanding of plasmon excitations.

title: Propagation of laser-generated GHz surface acoustic wavepackets in FeRh/MgO(001) below and above the antiferromagnetic-ferromagnetic phase transition
summary: Magnetoacoustic devices that harness the strong coupling between acoustic waves and magnons have emerged as a promising platform for energy-efficient spintronics. Laser-generated pulsed surface acoustic waves (SAWs) are particularly attractive for such applications, offering broadband frequency content up to the gigahertz (GHz) range, remote excitation without lithographic patterning, and surface localization for efficient on-chip integration. In this work, we present a comprehensive experimental study of laser-generated SAW pulses in the Fe49Rh51/MgO(001) system. A thin film of the near-equiatomic FeRh alloy serves both as an opto-acoustic transducer and as a mechanical load that modulates SAW propagation. The antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic phase transition in FeRh, occurring slightly above room temperature, is accompanied by abrupt changes in its elastic properties, enabling controlled modification of the SAW excitation efficiency and dispersion characteristics by tuning the sample temperature and laser fluence. Using 160 fs laser pulses for excitation and time-resolved Sagnac interferometry for detection, we evaluated key SAW parameters, including amplitude, spectral content, phase and group velocities, and their in-plane anisotropy. Particular emphasis is placed on the dispersion relation and its anisotropy, which govern the coherent interaction between phonons and magnons and are determined primarily by the FeRh film.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive experimental study of laser-generated pulsed surface acoustic waves (SAWs) in the Fe49Rh51/MgO(001) system for magnetoacoustic devices in energy-efficient spintronics.
AI.motivation: Magnetoacoustic devices harnessing acoustic wave-magnon coupling are promising for energy-efficient spintronics. Laser-generated SAWs offer broadband frequency content up to GHz, remote excitation without lithographic patterning, and efficient surface localization. The FeRh alloy's antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic phase transition near room temperature causes abrupt changes in elastic properties, enabling controlled modification of SAW excitation efficiency and dispersion characteristics.
AI.method: Using 160 fs laser pulses for excitation and time-resolved Sagnac interferometry for detection, the researchers evaluated key SAW parameters including amplitude, spectral content, phase and group velocities, and in-plane anisotropy. They studied the dispersion relation and its anisotropy, which govern coherent phonon-magnon interaction, by varying sample temperature and laser fluence.
AI.result: The Fe49Rh51 thin film serves as both an opto-acoustic transducer and mechanical load that modulates SAW propagation. The antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic phase transition in FeRh enables controlled tuning of SAW excitation efficiency and dispersion characteristics through temperature and laser fluence adjustments.
AI.conclusion: This work provides comprehensive experimental data on laser-generated SAW pulses in FeRh/MgO(001) system, establishing the foundation for coherent phonon-magnon interaction studies and practical magnetoacoustic devices for energy-efficient spintronic applications.

title: High-temperature charge-4e superconductivity in SU(4) interacting fermions
summary: The condensation of electron quartets, known as charge-4e superconductivity (SC), represents a novel quantum state of matter beyond the standard paradigm of Cooper pairing. However, concrete microscopic models realizing this phase in two dimensions remain a central challenge. Here, we introduce a non-engineered and sign-problem-free model, unambiguously demonstrating the emergence of a robust and high-temperature charge-4e SC phase using unbiased quantum Monte Carlo simulations. At zero temperature, the phase diagram reveals that charge-4e SC is the primary ground state in the strong-coupling regime. At finite temperature in the absence of charge-2e SC, we identify charge-4e SC through a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition, marked by a universal jump in the superfluid stiffness consistent with a condensate of charge 4e. Remarkably, the transition temperature Tc increases nearly linearly with interaction strength, providing a robust mechanism for high-Tc quartet superconductivity. Furthermore, spectral analysis reveals a prominent pseudogap above Tc arising from strong phase fluctuations. Our results establish a canonical and numerically exact model system for charge-4e superconductivity, offering crucial guidance for its realization in experimental platforms such as moiré materials and ultracold atomic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates the first unambiguous evidence of charge-4e superconductivity (quartet superconductivity) in a non-engineered, sign-problem-free 2D model using quantum Monte Carlo simulations. The charge-4e SC emerges as the primary ground state in the strong-coupling regime and exhibits a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition at finite temperature with a universal jump in superfluid stiffness.
AI.motivation: The condensation of electron quartets (charge-4e superconductivity) represents a novel quantum state beyond standard Cooper pairing (charge-2e). However, concrete microscopic models realizing this phase in two dimensions have remained a central challenge. The authors sought to find a robust, numerically exact model system for charge-4e superconductivity.
AI.method: The researchers used unbiased quantum Monte Carlo simulations to study a non-engineered and sign-problem-free model. They investigated both zero-temperature ground states and finite-temperature properties, including spectral analysis to study the pseudogap phase.
AI.result: At zero temperature, charge-4e SC emerges as the primary ground state in the strong-coupling regime. At finite temperature, a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition was identified with a universal jump in superfluid stiffness consistent with a condensate of charge 4e. The transition temperature Tc increases nearly linearly with interaction strength. A prominent pseudogap was observed above Tc arising from strong phase fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a canonical and numerically exact model system for charge-4e superconductivity. The findings offer crucial guidance for experimental realization in platforms such as moiré materials and ultracold atomic systems. The linear increase of Tc with interaction strength provides a robust mechanism for high-Tc quartet superconductivity.

title: Reversable phase transitions in ferroic two-dimensional Nb2O2I4 through optically excited coherent phonons
summary: We investigate optically induced phase transitions in the two-dimensional (2D) ferroelectric (FE) material Nb2O2I4 using real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT). Our results demonstrate that tailored laser pulses can activate specific coherent phonon modes. Specifically, the anharmonic atomic distortions of the A1-1 and A1-2 modes at the Γ-point facilitate the reversal of in-plane polarization. By fine-tuning laser parameters, additional phonon modes at both the Y and Γ points are excited. The resulting nonequilibrium atomic dynamics enable the formation of previously unreported ferroic phases, including three antiferroelectric (AFE) phases and one ferrielectric (FiE) phase. Notably, these optically induced phases can be reverted to the initial FE state using appropriate techniques. This controllable reversibility among multiple ferroic phases positions 2D Nb2O2I4 as a highly promising candidate for next-generation electronic storage applications.

AI.tldr: Using rt-TDDFT, this study demonstrates that tailored laser pulses can induce phase transitions in 2D Nb2O2I4 by activating coherent phonon modes, creating new ferroic phases (AFE and FiE) with controllable reversibility for electronic storage applications.
AI.motivation: Investigate optically induced phase transitions in the two-dimensional (2D) ferroelectric (FE) material Nb2O2I4
AI.method: Real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT)
AI.result: Tailored laser pulses can activate specific coherent phonon modes (A1-1 and A1-2 at Γ-point), facilitate polarization reversal, and form previously unreported ferroic phases including three antiferroelectric (AFE) phases and one ferrielectric (FiE) phase. These optically induced phases can be reverted to the initial FE state.
AI.conclusion: The controllable reversibility among multiple ferroic phases positions 2D Nb2O2I4 as a highly promising candidate for next-generation electronic storage applications.

title: Magneto-optical imaging of macroscopic altermagnetic domains in MnTe
summary: Altermagnets are a new class of magnets accompanying global time-reversal symmetry breaking (TRSB) without net magnetization. The TRSB results in formation of novel altermagnetic domains. Features of altermagnetic domains, in particular their responses to external stimuli, are essentially important but yet unexplored. Here, we report visualization of bulk altermagnetic domains in MnTe based on scanning magneto-optical Kerr-effect microscopy using telecom infrared wavelength. We found two distinct TRSB domains with large Kerr rotations that do not scale with its tiny bulk magnetization. We also revealed controllability and stability of domains against magnetic or thermal perturbations. Our first observation of altermagnetic domains using a laboratory-scale simple optical technique showing their movable nature provide firm bases for future fundamental and application studies of altermagnets.

AI.tldr: First visualization of bulk altermagnetic domains in MnTe using scanning magneto-optical Kerr-effect microscopy, revealing two distinct TRSB domains with large Kerr rotations that are controllable and stable against external perturbations.
AI.motivation: Altermagnets are a new class of magnets with global time-reversal symmetry breaking but no net magnetization. The formation of altermagnetic domains and their responses to external stimuli remain essentially unexplored, despite being important for understanding this new class of materials.
AI.method: The researchers used scanning magneto-optical Kerr-effect microscopy with telecom infrared wavelength to visualize bulk altermagnetic domains in MnTe.
AI.result: The study identified two distinct TRSB domains with large Kerr rotations that do not scale with the tiny bulk magnetization. The domains demonstrated controllability and stability against magnetic or thermal perturbations.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first observation of altermagnetic domains using a simple laboratory-scale optical technique, establishing a foundation for future fundamental studies and applications of altermagnets. The findings demonstrate that altermagnetic domains are movable and can be controlled, opening pathways for practical device applications.

title: Towards Non-van der Waals 2D Topological Insulators
summary: Non-van der Waals two-dimensional (2D) materials derived from strongly bonded non-layered crystals have recently emerged as a novel and rising platform for nanoscale research. While uncovering and tuning their (opto-)electronic, catalytic, and magnetic properties has been the focus of intense research, the impact of spin-orbit coupling (SOC) onto their electronic structure has not yet been explored in detail. Studying these effects is, however, particularly relevant due to their surface cation termination and the presence of heavy elements in several representative compounds. Here, we investigate the effect of SOC onto the electronic structure of 2D AgBiO3, NaBiO3, and SbTlO3. While the first two systems show negligible band renormalization upon inclusion of relativistic effects around the band gap, SbTlO3 showcases a large SOC induced splitting (229meV) for the lowest conduction bands associated with a band inversion. Substitution of Tl with Pb forming SbPbO3 brings the band-inverted feature to the Fermi level. Analysis of topological invariants and investigation of edge states of zig-zag and armchair ribbons within the 200meV gap confirms the topological nature of the band splitting. Our work thus establishes a foundation for the systematic study of robust non-van der Waals 2D topological insulators.

AI.tldr: This study investigates spin-orbit coupling (SOC) effects on the electronic structure of non-van der Waals 2D materials (AgBiO3, NaBiO3, SbTlO3, and SbPbO3) derived from strongly bonded non-layered crystals. While AgBiO3 and NaBiO3 show negligible SOC effects, SbTlO3 exhibits a large SOC-induced splitting of 229 meV with band inversion. Substituting Tl with Pb to form SbPbO3 brings the band-inverted feature to the Fermi level, and topological invariants and edge state calculations confirm the topological nature of the splitting within the 200 meV gap.
AI.motivation: While non-van der Waals 2D materials have emerged as a novel platform for nanoscale research with tunable opto-electronic, catalytic, and magnetic properties, the impact of spin-orbit coupling on their electronic structure has not been explored in detail. This is particularly relevant due to surface cation termination and presence of heavy elements in several compounds.
AI.method: The authors investigated SOC effects on the electronic structure of 2D AgBiO3, NaBiO3, and SbTlO3 using computational methods. They performed substitution of Tl with Pb to form SbPbO3, analyzed topological invariants, and investigated edge states of zig-zag and armchair ribbons within the energy gap.
AI.result: AgBiO3 and NaBiO3 show negligible band renormalization upon inclusion of relativistic effects around the band gap. SbTlO3 showcases a large SOC-induced splitting of 229 meV for the lowest conduction bands associated with band inversion. SbPbO3 brings the band-inverted feature to the Fermi level. Edge state calculations within the 200 meV gap confirm the topological nature of the band splitting.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a foundation for the systematic study of robust non-van der Waals 2D topological insulators, demonstrating that these materials can exhibit significant SOC effects and topological behavior despite being derived from strongly bonded non-layered crystals rather than traditional van der Waals materials.

title: Controllable highly oriented skyrmion track array in Fe3GaTe2
summary: Magnetic skyrmions are emerging as promising candidates for next-generation information technologies, while the realization of scalable skyrmion lattices with tailored configurations is essential for advancing fundamental skyrmion physics and developing future applications. Here we achieved the controllable generation and regulation of a large-area, highly oriented skyrmion track array (STA) in ferromagnetic Fe3GaTe2 using a vector magnetic field manipulation technique. The orientation and ordering of STA, along with the types and density of skyrmions, are precisely controlled by modulating parameters during the manipulation. The critical roles of in-plane magnetic fields and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction in STA generation is further confirmed by micromagnetic simulation. Our findings develop a strategy for engineering large-area and highly-oriented skyrmion configurations, offering a new pathway for the future application of next-generation spintronic and information technologies.
