title: Disentangling the ferroelectric phases of epitaxial hafnia
summary: Since its discovery, ferroelectric hafnia has been extensively studied due to its CMOS-compatibility and ability to remain polarized at sub-10 nm thicknesses. The ferroelectric behaviour is generally attributed to a polar orthorhombic (OIII) phase. However, a second polar phase with rhombohedral symmetry (R-phase) has also been reported in epitaxial films. The nature of the R-phase remains disputed due to the subtle differences with the OIII-phase when probed by standard thin film characterisation techniques. Given the functional properties of ferroelectrics are crucially determined by the crystal symmetry, resolving this matter is imperative. In this work, we settle the controversy through extensive 3D reciprocal space surveys made possible via synchrotron-based grazing incidence diffraction from epitaxial films of both phases. These experiments, together with direct comparison of their temperature dependence and electrical responses, conclusively establish them as two distinct phases and provide insight into their key characteristics.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the controversy between two polar phases (OIII orthorhombic and R-phase rhombohedral) in ferroelectric hafnia by using synchrotron-based grazing incidence diffraction, confirming they are distinct phases with different crystal symmetries and properties.
AI.motivation: The nature of the R-phase in ferroelectric hafnia remained disputed due to subtle differences from the OIII-phase when probed by standard thin film characterization techniques. Since functional properties of ferroelectrics are crucially determined by crystal symmetry, resolving this controversy was imperative.
AI.method: The researchers used extensive 3D reciprocal space surveys via synchrotron-based grazing incidence diffraction from epitaxial films of both phases, combined with temperature-dependent measurements and electrical response analysis.
AI.result: The study conclusively demonstrated that the OIII and R-phases are two distinct phases, not the same phase as previously debated. The R-phase was confirmed to have rhombohedral symmetry with distinct temperature dependence and electrical responses compared to the orthorhombic OIII-phase.
AI.conclusion: The study conclusively establishes the OIII and R phases as two distinct phases in ferroelectric hafnia, resolving the controversy. The R-phase is confirmed to have rhombohedral symmetry, different from the orthorhombic OIII-phase, with distinct temperature dependence and electrical responses.

title: Fully Atomic-Layer-Deposited Vertical Complementary FeRAM with Ultra-High 2Pr > 100 uC/cm2 and High Endurance > 1E10 cycles
summary: A limited remanent polarization (Pr) in HfO2-based FeRAM remains a key obstacle to density scaling and reliability, while material and process optimizations offer only incremental improvements. This limitation fundamentally originates from the thickness-constrained switchable polarization and the intrinsic polarization ceiling of HfO2-based ferroelectrics. Here, we propose an all-ALD-grown vertical complementary FeRAM (VCF) architecture, in which the top and bottom stacked FeRAM cells maintain complementary polarization. This complementary dipole configuration converts the readout from a single-layer polarization response into a differential polarization summation, thereby amplifying the effective charge window without increasing the switching field of each individual layer or incurring area overhead. Viewed from top to bottom, an "up-down" polarization pair stores logic '1', whereas a "down-up" pair stores logic '0'. Using a complementary polarization write-read scheme, the VCF achieves an effective differential polarization above 100 uC/cm^2 and retains above 90 uC/cm^2 after 1e10 switching cycles without electrical breakdown. Robust retention (longer than 1e4 s at 85 degC) and strong disturb immunity are demonstrated, with an effective differential polarization above 80 uC/cm^2 under a V/3 scheme after 1e6 disturb pulses. Array-level operation is validated in a 5 x 5 selector-free crosspoint array. The performance enhancement of the VCF arises from the co-optimization of the all-ALD-grown process, device architecture, and operation scheme, enabling high density, a wide memory window, and strong reliability for scalable FeRAM integration.

This differential polarization approach achieves over 100 uC/cm^2 effective polarization, excellent retention (1e4s at 85°C), high endurance (1e10 cycles), and strong disturb immunity, validated in a 5x5 crosspoint array.

title: Lattice dynamics and complete polarization analysis of Raman-active modes in LaInO$_3$
summary: In this study, we present a comprehensive analysis of the Raman active phonon modes in orthorhombic LaInO$_3$ based on a combination of polarization-angle resolved Raman spectroscopy and density functional theory calculations. By using backscattering from multiple crystallographic surface orientations and employing a full symmetry analysis, we identify and assign most of the Raman-active $Γ$-point phonons to their irreducible representations of the D$_{\rm{2h}}$ point group. A multidimensional hyperspectral fitting procedure allows us to extract the relative Raman tensor elements from the angular dependence of the scattering intensities, even for strongly overlapping modes. First-principles calculations yield the phonon dispersion along high-symmetry directions, the phonon densities of states, and atomic displacement patterns, which are found to be in good agreement with the experimental mode frequencies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive Raman spectroscopy and DFT study of phonon modes in orthorhombic LaInO3, identifying Raman-active phonons and assigning them to D2h point group representations through polarization-angle resolved measurements and first-principles calculations.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to comprehensively analyze the Raman active phonon modes in orthorhombic LaInO3, which required both experimental characterization and theoretical calculations to understand the phonon behavior in this material.
AI.method: The researchers used polarization-angle resolved Raman spectroscopy with backscattering from multiple crystallographic surface orientations, combined with density functional theory (DFT) calculations. They employed a full symmetry analysis and developed a multidimensional hyperspectral fitting procedure to extract relative Raman tensor elements from angular dependence of scattering intensities, even for overlapping modes.
AI.result: They identified and assigned most Raman-active Γ-point phonons to their irreducible representations of the D2h point group. First-principles calculations yielded phonon dispersion along high-symmetry directions, phonon densities of states, and atomic displacement patterns, which showed good agreement with experimental mode frequencies.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully identified and assigned most Raman-active Γ-point phonons in orthorhombic LaInO3 to their irreducible representations of the D2h point group. The combination of experimental Raman spectroscopy and DFT calculations showed good agreement, validating the approach.

title: Optimal spin-qubit hallmarks of sulfur-vacancy defects in 4H-SiC: Design from first principles
summary: By applying our methodology, we propose a defect in 4H-SiC which combines a Si vacancy and a C atom substituted with S (VSiSC) to have a spin-triplet ground state with the spin qubit functionality. Our calculations confirm that all configurations of the defect have a dynamically and thermodynamically stable triplet ground state and higher energy singlet states, essential for the spin-qubit polarization cycle. From GW calculations, we found that the electronic states associated with the defect form sharp and isolated peaks within the band gap for both triplet and singlet states. Further Bethe-Salpeter-equation calculations show that all considered configurations have intense optical excitations in the near infrared spectrum range. Analysis of the excitation energies and rates indicate that the VSiSC defect can be an excellent optically controlled spin qubit. Crucially, the host elements and the dopant have high-abundance isotopes with zero nuclear spin ensuring high spin-coherence time of the qubit.

AI.tldr: First-principles calculations predict that the VSiSC defect (Si vacancy + S-substituted C) in 4H-SiC exhibits a stable spin-triplet ground state with isolated electronic states in the band gap and intense near-infrared optical excitations, making it an excellent candidate for optically controlled spin qubits with long coherence times due to zero-nuclear-spin isotopes.
AI.motivation: Developing reliable spin qubits for quantum computing applications requires identifying defects with appropriate electronic properties, including stable spin states, suitable optical transitions, and long coherence times. The search for defects in wide-bandgap semiconductors like SiC that can serve as functional spin qubits drives this research.
AI.method: The study employed first-principles calculations using GW methodology to determine electronic structure, followed by Bethe-Salpeter-equation calculations to compute optical excitation properties. Multiple defect configurations were analyzed to assess dynamic and thermodynamic stability.
AI.result: The VSiSC defect exhibits a spin-triplet ground state across all configurations with dynamically and thermodynamically stable triplet states and higher-energy singlet states essential for spin-qubit polarization cycles. GW calculations reveal sharp, isolated electronic states within the band gap for both triplet and singlet configurations. Bethe-Salpeter calculations demonstrate intense optical excitations in the near-infrared spectrum. The host elements (Si and C) and dopant (S) have high-abundance isotopes with zero nuclear spin, enabling long spin-coherence times.
AI.conclusion: The VSiSC defect in 4H-SiC emerges as a highly promising candidate for optically controlled spin qubits, combining stable triplet ground state, favorable optical properties in the near-infrared range, and isotopic composition that naturally supports long spin-coherence times. The defect's dynamically and thermodynamically stable triplet state makes it particularly attractive for practical qubit implementation.
